subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html:
Explain the following observations:

  1. Transition elements generally form coloured compounds.
  2. Zinc is not regarded as a transition element.
question_text: Explain the following observations: Transition elements generally form coloured compounds. Zinc is not regarded as a transition element.
explanation: Transition elements are coloured because of the absorption of radiation from visible light region to promote an electron from one of the d-orbital to another. A transition metal is one which forms one or more stable ions which have incompletely filled d orbitals. But, Zinc ( 30 Zn = 18 [Ar]3d 10 4s 2 ) has completely filled d-orbitals in its atomic as well as in its common oxidation state (Zn 2+ state). Therefore, on the basis of the definition of transition elements, Zinc( 30 Zn) is not regarded as transition element.
solution: Transition elements are coloured because of the absorption of radiation from visible light region to promote an electron from one of the d-orbital to another. A transition metal is one which forms one or more stable ions which have incompletely filled d orbitals. But, Zinc ( 30 Zn = 18 [Ar]3d 10 4s 2 ) has completely filled d-orbitals in its atomic as well as in its common oxidation state (Zn 2+ state). Therefore, on the basis of the definition of transition elements, Zinc( 30 Zn) is not regarded as transition element.
solution_html:
1. Transition elements are coloured because of the absorption of radiation from visible light region to promote an electron from one of the d-orbital to another.
  2. A transition metal is one which forms one or more stable ions which have incompletely filled d orbitals. But, Zinc (30Zn = 18[Ar]3d104s2) has completely filled d-orbitals in its atomic as well as in its common oxidation state (Zn2+ state). Therefore, on the basis of the definition of transition elements, Zinc(30Zn) is not regarded as transition element.

question_html:
Complete and balance the following chemical equations:

  1. {tex}{MnO}_{4}^{-}+{C}_{2} {O}_{4}^{2-}+{H}^{+} \longrightarrow{/tex}
  2. {tex}{KMnO}_{4} \xrightarrow[513 {~K}]{\text { Heat }}{/tex}
  3. {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}^{2-}+{H}_{2} {~S}+{H}^{+} \longrightarrow{/tex}
question_text: Complete and balance the following chemical equations: {tex}{MnO}_{4}^{-}+{C}_{2} {O}_{4}^{2-}+{H}^{+} \longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{KMnO}_{4} \xrightarrow[513 {~K}]{\text { Heat }}{/tex} {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}^{2-}+{H}_{2} {~S}+{H}^{+} \longrightarrow{/tex}
explanation: {tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} + {tex}5 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} + 16H + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Mn 2+ + 8H 2 O + 10CO 2 On strong heating, KMnO4 decomposes to potassium manganate. {tex}\mathop {2KMn{O_2}}\limits_{(Purple)} \xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {{K_2}Mn{O_4}}\limits_{(Green)} + Mn{O_2} + {O_2}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}{/tex} + 3H 2 S + 8H + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Cr 3+ + 3S + 7H 2 O
solution: {tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} + {tex}5 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} + 16H + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Mn 2+ + 8H 2 O + 10CO 2 On strong heating, KMnO4 decomposes to potassium manganate. {tex}\mathop {2KMn{O_2}}\limits_{(Purple)} \xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {{K_2}Mn{O_4}}\limits_{(Green)} + Mn{O_2} + {O_2}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}{/tex} + 3H 2 S + 8H + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Cr 3+ + 3S + 7H 2 O
solution_html:
1. {tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} + {tex}5 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} + 16H+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Mn2+ + 8H2O + 10CO2
  2. On strong heating, KMnO4 decomposes to potassium manganate.
    {tex}\mathop {2KMn{O_2}}\limits_{(Purple)} \xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {{K_2}Mn{O_4}}\limits_{(Green)} + Mn{O_2} + {O_2}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}{/tex}  + 3H2S + 8H+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Cr3+ + 3S + 7H2O

question_html: Which of the following transition metals does not show variable oxidation state?

options.explanation: In case of scandium the number of electrons in the 3d and 4s orbital is 1 and 2 respectively. question_html: When {tex}{FeCr}_{2} {O}_{4}{/tex} is fused with {tex}{Na}_{2} {CO}_{3}{/tex} in the presence of air it gives a yellow solution of compound (A). Compound (A) on acidification gives compound (B). Compound (B) on reaction with KCl forms an orange coloured (C). An acidified solution of compound (C) oxidises iodide to (D). Identify (A), (B), (C) and (D).
question_text: When {tex}{FeCr}_{2} {O}_{4}{/tex} is fused with {tex}{Na}_{2} {CO}_{3}{/tex} in the presence of air it gives a yellow solution of compound (A). Compound (A) on acidification gives compound (B). Compound (B) on reaction with KCl forms an orange coloured (C). An acidified solution of compound (C) oxidises iodide to (D). Identify (A), (B), (C) and (D).
explanation: A = Na 2 CrO 4 /Sodium chromate B = Na 2 Cr 2 O 7 /Sodium dichromate C = K 2 Cr 2 O 7 /Potassium dichromate D = I 2 /Iodine
solution: A = Na 2 CrO 4 /Sodium chromate B = Na 2 Cr 2 O 7 /Sodium dichromate C = K 2 Cr 2 O 7 /Potassium dichromate D = I 2 /Iodine
solution_html:
A = Na2CrO4/Sodium chromate
B = Na2Cr2O7/Sodium dichromate
C = K2Cr2O7/Potassium dichromate
D = I2/Iodine

question_html:
1. {tex}{E}_{\left({Mn}^{2+} / {Mn}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} is -1.18 V. Why is this value highly negative in comparison to neighbouring d-block elements?
  2. What is lanthanoid contraction?
  3. {tex}{Zn}, {Cd}{/tex} and Hg are soft metals. Why?
question_text: {tex}{E}_{\left({Mn}^{2+} / {Mn}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} is -1.18 V. Why is this value highly negative in comparison to neighbouring d-block elements? What is lanthanoid contraction? {tex}{Zn}, {Cd}{/tex} and Hg are soft metals. Why?
explanation: {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} results in the half-filled {tex}\left(d^{5}\right){/tex} configuration which has extra stability. The steady decrease in atomic and ionic radii in lanthanoid series. Due to weak interatomic interactions / weak metallic bonds.
solution: {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} results in the half-filled {tex}\left(d^{5}\right){/tex} configuration which has extra stability. The steady decrease in atomic and ionic radii in lanthanoid series. Due to weak interatomic interactions / weak metallic bonds.
solution_html:
1. {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} results in the half-filled {tex}\left(d^{5}\right){/tex} configuration which has extra stability.
  2. The steady decrease in atomic and ionic radii in lanthanoid series.
  3. Due to weak interatomic interactions / weak metallic bonds.

question_html:
Answer the following questions:

  1. Name two elements of 3d series for which the third ionisation enthalpies are quite high.
  2. Out of {tex}{KMnO}_4{/tex} and {tex}{K}_2 {MnO}_4{/tex}, which one is paramagnetic and why?
  3. Write any one consequence of lanthanoid contraction.
  4. How do you prepare potassium manganate from pyrolusite ore?
  5. Why is the ability of oxygen more than fluorine to stabilise higher oxidation states of transition metals?
question_text: Answer the following questions: Name two elements of 3d series for which the third ionisation enthalpies are quite high. Out of {tex}{KMnO}_4{/tex} and {tex}{K}_2 {MnO}_4{/tex} , which one is paramagnetic and why? Write any one consequence of lanthanoid contraction. How do you prepare potassium manganate from pyrolusite ore? Why is the ability of oxygen more than fluorine to stabilise higher oxidation states of transition metals?
explanation: {tex}{Mn}, {Zn}, {Ni}, {Cu}{/tex} {tex}{K}_{2} {MnO}_{4}{/tex} , due to presence of one unpaired electron Similar radii of 4d and 5d series elements/ similar properties/ difficulty in separation of lanthanoids It is prepared by fusion of {tex}{MnO}_{2}{/tex} with an alkali metal hydroxide and an oxidising agent / {tex}2 {MnO}_{2}+4 {KOH}+{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 {~K}_{2} {MnO}_{4}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} because of the ability of oxygen to form multiple bonds with metal
solution: {tex}{Mn}, {Zn}, {Ni}, {Cu}{/tex} {tex}{K}_{2} {MnO}_{4}{/tex} , due to presence of one unpaired electron Similar radii of 4d and 5d series elements/ similar properties/ difficulty in separation of lanthanoids It is prepared by fusion of {tex}{MnO}_{2}{/tex} with an alkali metal hydroxide and an oxidising agent / {tex}2 {MnO}_{2}+4 {KOH}+{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 {~K}_{2} {MnO}_{4}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} because of the ability of oxygen to form multiple bonds with metal
solution_html:
1. {tex}{Mn}, {Zn}, {Ni}, {Cu}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{K}_{2} {MnO}_{4}{/tex}, due to presence of one unpaired electron
  3. Similar radii of 4d and 5d series elements/ similar properties/ difficulty in separation of lanthanoids
  4. It is prepared by fusion of {tex}{MnO}_{2}{/tex} with an alkali metal hydroxide and an oxidising agent /{tex}2 {MnO}_{2}+4 {KOH}+{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 {~K}_{2} {MnO}_{4}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex}
  5. because of the ability of oxygen to form multiple bonds with metal

  2. Complete the following ionic equations:
    1. {tex} 5 {SO}_3{ }^{2-}+2 {MnO}_4{ }^{-}+6 {H}^{+} \longrightarrow {/tex}
    2. {tex} 2 {MnO}_4{ }^{-}+{H}_2 {O}+{I}^{-} \longrightarrow {/tex}
question_text: Account for the following: The {tex}{E}_{{Mn}^{2+} / {Mn}}^{\circ}{/tex} value for manganese is highly negative, whereas {tex}{E}_{{Mn}^{3+} / {Mn}^{2+}}^{\circ}{/tex} is highly positive. Actinoids show wide range of oxidation states. Transition metals have high melting points. Complete the following ionic equations: {tex} 5 {SO}_3{ }^{2-}+2 {MnO}_4{ }^{-}+6 {H}^{+} \longrightarrow {/tex} {tex} 2 {MnO}_4{ }^{-}+{H}_2 {O}+{I}^{-} \longrightarrow {/tex}
explanation: Because {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} is more stable than {tex}{Mn}^{3+}{/tex} due to extra stable half-filled {tex}{d}^{5}{/tex} configuration. Due to comparable energies of 5f, 6d and 7s orbitals Due to the involvement of greater number of electrons from ( {tex}{n}-1{/tex} )d in addition to the ns electrons in the inter-atomic metallic bonding. {tex}5 {SO}_{3}^{2-}+2 {MnO}_{4}^{-}+6 {H}^{+} \longrightarrow {/tex} {tex}2 {Mn}^{2+}+3 {H}_{2} {O}+5 {SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} {tex}2 {MnO}_{4}^{-}+{H}_{2} {O}+{I}^{-} \longrightarrow {/tex} {tex}2 {MnO}_{2}+2 {OH}^{-}+I {O}_{3}^{-}{/tex}
solution: Because {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} is more stable than {tex}{Mn}^{3+}{/tex} due to extra stable half-filled {tex}{d}^{5}{/tex} configuration. Due to comparable energies of 5f, 6d and 7s orbitals Due to the involvement of greater number of electrons from ( {tex}{n}-1{/tex} )d in addition to the ns electrons in the inter-atomic metallic bonding. {tex}5 {SO}_{3}^{2-}+2 {MnO}_{4}^{-}+6 {H}^{+} \longrightarrow {/tex} {tex}2 {Mn}^{2+}+3 {H}_{2} {O}+5 {SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} {tex}2 {MnO}_{4}^{-}+{H}_{2} {O}+{I}^{-} \longrightarrow {/tex} {tex}2 {MnO}_{2}+2 {OH}^{-}+I {O}_{3}^{-}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Because {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} is more stable than {tex}{Mn}^{3+}{/tex} due to extra stable half-filled {tex}{d}^{5}{/tex} configuration.
    2. Due to comparable energies of 5f, 6d and 7s orbitals
    3. Due to the involvement of greater number of electrons from ({tex}{n}-1{/tex})d in addition to the ns electrons in the inter-atomic metallic bonding.
    1. {tex}5 {SO}_{3}^{2-}+2 {MnO}_{4}^{-}+6 {H}^{+} \longrightarrow {/tex} {tex}2 {Mn}^{2+}+3 {H}_{2} {O}+5 {SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex}
    2. {tex}2 {MnO}_{4}^{-}+{H}_{2} {O}+{I}^{-} \longrightarrow {/tex} {tex}2 {MnO}_{2}+2 {OH}^{-}+I {O}_{3}^{-}{/tex}

question_text: Of the {tex}{d}^{4}{/tex} species, {tex}{Cr}^{2+}{/tex} is strongly reducing while {tex}{Mn}^{3+}{/tex} is strongly oxidising. Why? Write two consequences of lanthanoid contraction. Which element of 3d series has lowest enthalpy of atomisation and why?
explanation: Because {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} is more stable due to stable {tex}3 {~d}^{5}{/tex} configuration whereas {tex}{Cr}^{3+}{/tex} is more stable due to stable {tex}{t}_{2 {~g}}{ }^{3}{/tex} configuration. Similar atomic radii of 4d and 5d series elements Separation of lanthanoids becomes difficult. Zinc, due to weak interatomic interactions / Weak metallic bond.
solution: Because {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} is more stable due to stable {tex}3 {~d}^{5}{/tex} configuration whereas {tex}{Cr}^{3+}{/tex} is more stable due to stable {tex}{t}_{2 {~g}}{ }^{3}{/tex} configuration. Similar atomic radii of 4d and 5d series elements Separation of lanthanoids becomes difficult. Zinc, due to weak interatomic interactions / Weak metallic bond.
solution_html:
1. Because {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} is more stable due to stable {tex}3 {~d}^{5}{/tex} configuration whereas {tex}{Cr}^{3+}{/tex} is more stable due to stable {tex}{t}_{2 {~g}}{ }^{3}{/tex} configuration.
  2. Similar atomic radii of 4d and 5d series elements
    Separation of lanthanoids becomes difficult.
  3. Zinc, due to weak interatomic interactions / Weak metallic bond.

question_html:
1. Complete and balance the following chemical equations:
    1. {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}^{2-}({aq})+{H}_{2} {~S}({~g})+{H}^{+} \longrightarrow{/tex}
    2. {tex}{Cu}^{2+}({aq})+{I}^{-}({aq}) \longrightarrow{/tex}
  2. Give reasons:
    1. Separation of mixture of lanthanoids is difficult.
    2. Chromium has higher melting point than manganese.
    3. It is difficult to obtain oxidation state greater than two for Zn.
question_text: Complete and balance the following chemical equations: {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}^{2-}({aq})+{H}_{2} {~S}({~g})+{H}^{+} \longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{Cu}^{2+}({aq})+{I}^{-}({aq}) \longrightarrow{/tex} Give reasons: Separation of mixture of lanthanoids is difficult. Chromium has higher melting point than manganese. It is difficult to obtain oxidation state greater than two for Zn.
explanation: {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}{ }^{2-}+8 {H}^{+}+3 {H}_{2} {~S} {/tex} {tex}\rightarrow 2 {Cr}^{3+}+7 {H}_{2} {O}+3 {~S}{/tex} {tex}2 {Cu}^{2+}+4 {I}^{-} \rightarrow {Cu}_{2} {I}_{2({~s})}+{I}_{2}{/tex} Due to lanthanoid contraction. Due to more number of unpaired electrons from ns and {tex}({n}-1) {d}{/tex} in chromium as compared to manganese. Due to the higher value of the third ionisation enthalpy of zinc.
solution: {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}{ }^{2-}+8 {H}^{+}+3 {H}_{2} {~S} {/tex} {tex}\rightarrow 2 {Cr}^{3+}+7 {H}_{2} {O}+3 {~S}{/tex} {tex}2 {Cu}^{2+}+4 {I}^{-} \rightarrow {Cu}_{2} {I}_{2({~s})}+{I}_{2}{/tex} Due to lanthanoid contraction. Due to more number of unpaired electrons from ns and {tex}({n}-1) {d}{/tex} in chromium as compared to manganese. Due to the higher value of the third ionisation enthalpy of zinc.
solution_html:
1. {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}{ }^{2-}+8 {H}^{+}+3 {H}_{2} {~S} {/tex} {tex}\rightarrow 2 {Cr}^{3+}+7 {H}_{2} {O}+3 {~S}{/tex}
    2. {tex}2 {Cu}^{2+}+4 {I}^{-} \rightarrow {Cu}_{2} {I}_{2({~s})}+{I}_{2}{/tex}
    1. Due to lanthanoid contraction.
    2. Due to more number of unpaired electrons from ns and {tex}({n}-1) {d}{/tex} in chromium as compared to manganese.
    3. Due to the higher value of the third ionisation enthalpy of zinc.

question_html:
1. Write the chemical equations involved in the preparation of potassium permanganate from pyrolusite ore ({tex}{MnO}_{2}{/tex}).
  2. Give reasons for the following:
    1. {tex}{Sc}{/tex} (Atomic No. 21) salts are white.
    2. {tex}{E}^o_{\left.{Cu}^{2+}\right|{{Cu}} }{/tex} is positive whereas {tex}{E}_{\left.{Mn}^{2+}\right|{{Mn}}}^{0}{/tex} is negative.
    3. Transition metals have high enthalpies of atomisation.
question_text: Write the chemical equations involved in the preparation of potassium permanganate from pyrolusite ore ( {tex}{MnO}_{2}{/tex} ). Give reasons for the following: {tex}{Sc}{/tex} (Atomic No. 21) salts are white. {tex}{E}^o_{\left.{Cu}^{2+}\right|{{Cu}} }{/tex} is positive whereas {tex}{E}_{\left.{Mn}^{2+}\right|{{Mn}}}^{0}{/tex} is negative. Transition metals have high enthalpies of atomisation.
explanation: {tex}2 {MnO}_{2}+4 {KOH}+{O}_{2}{/tex} {tex} \rightarrow 2 {~K}_{2} {MnO}_{4}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} {tex}3 {MnO}_{4}^{2-}+4 {H}^{+} {/tex} {tex}\rightarrow 2 {MnO}_{4}^{-}+{MnO}_{2}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} Due to the absence of unpaired electrons in d-orbitals./no d-d transition. Due to the low {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} and high {tex}\Delta_{{a}} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} of {tex}{Cu}^{2+}{/tex} , whereas {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} has lower {tex}\Delta_{{a}} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} as well as lower {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} /Due to the low {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} and high {tex}\Delta_{{aa}} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} of {tex}{Cu}^{2+}{/tex} , whereas {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} is highly stable due to its {tex}3 {~d}^{5}{/tex} configuration. Due to the presence of a greater number of unpaired electrons, resulting in strong interatomic interactions/metallic bonding.
solution: {tex}2 {MnO}_{2}+4 {KOH}+{O}_{2}{/tex} {tex} \rightarrow 2 {~K}_{2} {MnO}_{4}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} {tex}3 {MnO}_{4}^{2-}+4 {H}^{+} {/tex} {tex}\rightarrow 2 {MnO}_{4}^{-}+{MnO}_{2}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} Due to the absence of unpaired electrons in d-orbitals./no d-d transition. Due to the low {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} and high {tex}\Delta_{{a}} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} of {tex}{Cu}^{2+}{/tex} , whereas {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} has lower {tex}\Delta_{{a}} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} as well as lower {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} /Due to the low {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} and high {tex}\Delta_{{aa}} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} of {tex}{Cu}^{2+}{/tex} , whereas {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} is highly stable due to its {tex}3 {~d}^{5}{/tex} configuration. Due to the presence of a greater number of unpaired electrons, resulting in strong interatomic interactions/metallic bonding.
solution_html:
1. {tex}2 {MnO}_{2}+4 {KOH}+{O}_{2}{/tex} {tex} \rightarrow 2 {~K}_{2} {MnO}_{4}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex}
    {tex}3 {MnO}_{4}^{2-}+4 {H}^{+} {/tex} {tex}\rightarrow 2 {MnO}_{4}^{-}+{MnO}_{2}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex}
  2.  
    1. Due to the absence of unpaired electrons in d-orbitals./no d-d transition.
    2. Due to the low {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} and high {tex}\Delta_{{a}} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} of {tex}{Cu}^{2+}{/tex}, whereas {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} has lower {tex}\Delta_{{a}} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} as well as lower {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} {H}^{\circ}{/tex}/Due to the low {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} and high {tex}\Delta_{{aa}} {H}^{\circ}{/tex} of {tex}{Cu}^{2+}{/tex}, whereas {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} is highly stable due to its {tex}3 {~d}^{5}{/tex} configuration.
    3. Due to the presence of a greater number of unpaired electrons, resulting in strong interatomic interactions/metallic bonding.

subject: Chemistry

question_html:
Give explanation for each of the following observations:

  1. With the same d-orbital configuration ({tex}{d}^{4}{/tex}), {tex}{Mn}^{3+}{/tex} ion is an oxidising agent whereas {tex}{Cr}^{2+}{/tex} ion is a reducing agent.
  2. Actinoid contraction is greater from element to element than that among lanthanoids.
  3. Transition metals form large number of interstitial compounds with {tex}{H}, {B}, {C}{/tex} and N.
question_text: Give explanation for each of the following observations: With the same d-orbital configuration ( {tex}{d}^{4}{/tex} ), {tex}{Mn}^{3+}{/tex} ion is an oxidising agent whereas {tex}{Cr}^{2+}{/tex} ion is a reducing agent. Actinoid contraction is greater from element to element than that among lanthanoids. Transition metals form large number of interstitial compounds with {tex}{H}, {B}, {C}{/tex} and N.
explanation: Change from {tex}{Mn}^{3+}{/tex} to {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} results in extra stable half filled {tex}{d}^{5}{/tex} configuration. {tex}{Cr}^{2+}{/tex} is reducing as its configuration changes from {tex}{d}^{4}{/tex} to {tex}{d}^{3}{/tex} which is stable half filled {tex}{t}_{2 g}{ }^{3}{/tex} configuration. Due to poorer shielding offered by {tex}5 f{/tex} electrons than {tex}4 f{/tex} . {tex}{H}, {B}, {C}{/tex} and N atoms being small in size get trapped inside the crystal lattices of transition metals.
solution: Change from {tex}{Mn}^{3+}{/tex} to {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} results in extra stable half filled {tex}{d}^{5}{/tex} configuration. {tex}{Cr}^{2+}{/tex} is reducing as its configuration changes from {tex}{d}^{4}{/tex} to {tex}{d}^{3}{/tex} which is stable half filled {tex}{t}_{2 g}{ }^{3}{/tex} configuration. Due to poorer shielding offered by {tex}5 f{/tex} electrons than {tex}4 f{/tex} . {tex}{H}, {B}, {C}{/tex} and N atoms being small in size get trapped inside the crystal lattices of transition metals.
solution_html:
1. Change from {tex}{Mn}^{3+}{/tex} to {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} results in extra stable half filled {tex}{d}^{5}{/tex} configuration.
    {tex}{Cr}^{2+}{/tex} is reducing as its configuration changes from {tex}{d}^{4}{/tex} to {tex}{d}^{3}{/tex} which is stable half filled {tex}{t}_{2 g}{ }^{3}{/tex} configuration.
  2. Due to poorer shielding offered by {tex}5 f{/tex} electrons than {tex}4 f{/tex}.
  3. {tex}{H}, {B}, {C}{/tex} and N atoms being small in size get trapped inside the crystal lattices of transition metals.

Answer the following questions:

  1. What is 'Misch metal'? Give its one use.
  2. Write the formula of an oxoanion of chromium in which it shows the oxidation state equal to its group number.
  3. Why does Vanadium pentoxide {tex}\left({V}_{2} {O}_{5}\right){/tex} act as a catalyst?
  4. Why do transition elements have high enthalpies of atomisation?
  5. How do you prepare {tex}{Na}_{2} {Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}{/tex} from {tex}{Na}_{2} {CrO}_{4}{/tex}?
question_text: Answer the following questions: What is 'Misch metal'? Give its one use. Write the formula of an oxoanion of chromium in which it shows the oxidation state equal to its group number. Why does Vanadium pentoxide {tex}\left({V}_{2} {O}_{5}\right){/tex} act as a catalyst? Why do transition elements have high enthalpies of atomisation? How do you prepare {tex}{Na}_{2} {Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}{/tex} from {tex}{Na}_{2} {CrO}_{4}{/tex} ?
explanation: An alloy of lanthanoid / an alloy of lanthanoid and iron with traces of S, C, Ca and Al. used in making bullets/shells/ lighter flint {tex}{CrO}_{4}{ }^{2-} / {Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}{ }^{2-}{/tex} variable oxidation state of vanadium / large surface area /Complex formation Because of large number of unpaired electrons in their atoms they have stronger interatomic interaction or strong metallic bonding by acidification of {tex}{Na}_{2} {CrO}_{4} /{/tex} {tex}2 {Na}_{2} {CrO}_{4}+2 {H}^{*} \rightarrow {Na}_{2} {Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}+2 {Na}^{+}+{H}_{2} {O}{/tex}
solution: An alloy of lanthanoid / an alloy of lanthanoid and iron with traces of S, C, Ca and Al. used in making bullets/shells/ lighter flint {tex}{CrO}_{4}{ }^{2-} / {Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}{ }^{2-}{/tex} variable oxidation state of vanadium / large surface area /Complex formation Because of large number of unpaired electrons in their atoms they have stronger interatomic interaction or strong metallic bonding by acidification of {tex}{Na}_{2} {CrO}_{4} /{/tex} {tex}2 {Na}_{2} {CrO}_{4}+2 {H}^{*} \rightarrow {Na}_{2} {Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}+2 {Na}^{+}+{H}_{2} {O}{/tex}
solution_html:
• An alloy of lanthanoid / an alloy of lanthanoid and iron with traces of S, C, Ca and Al.
    • used in making bullets/shells/ lighter flint
  2. {tex}{CrO}_{4}{ }^{2-} / {Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}{ }^{2-}{/tex}
  3. variable oxidation state of vanadium / large surface area /Complex formation
  4. Because of large number of unpaired electrons in their atoms they have stronger interatomic interaction or strong metallic bonding
  5. by acidification of {tex}{Na}_{2} {CrO}_{4} /{/tex}
    {tex}2 {Na}_{2} {CrO}_{4}+2 {H}^{*} \rightarrow {Na}_{2} {Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}+2 {Na}^{+}+{H}_{2} {O}{/tex}

1. Account for the following:
    1. Orange colour of {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}{ }^{2-}{/tex} ion changes to yellow when treated with an alkali.
    2. {tex}{Zn}, {Cd}{/tex} and Hg are non-transition elements.
    3. {tex}{E}^{\circ}{/tex} value for {tex}{Mn}^{3+} / {Mn}^{2+}{/tex} couple is highly positive {tex}(+1 \cdot 57 {~V}){/tex} as compared to {tex}{Cr}^{3+} / {Cr}^{2+}{/tex}.
  2. What happens when:
    1. Manganate ion undergoes disproportionation reaction in acidic medium?
    2. {tex}{KMnO}_{4}{/tex} is heated?
question_text: Account for the following: Orange colour of {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}{ }^{2-}{/tex} ion changes to yellow when treated with an alkali. {tex}{Zn}, {Cd}{/tex} and Hg are non-transition elements. {tex}{E}^{\circ}{/tex} value for {tex}{Mn}^{3+} / {Mn}^{2+}{/tex} couple is highly positive {tex}(+1 \cdot 57 {~V}){/tex} as compared to {tex}{Cr}^{3+} / {Cr}^{2+}{/tex} . What happens when: Manganate ion undergoes disproportionation reaction in acidic medium? {tex}{KMnO}_{4}{/tex} is heated?
explanation: Due to formation of chromate {tex}/ {CrO}_{4}{ }^{2-}{/tex} ion Due to completely filled d-orbitals in ground state as well as oxidised state. Because {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} is more stable due to stable {tex}3 {~d}^{5}{/tex} configuration whereas {tex}{Cr}^{3+}{/tex} is more stable due to stable {tex}{t}_{2 g}{ }^{3}{/tex} configuration. it changes to permanaganate ion / {tex}{MnO}_{4}{ }^{-}{/tex} is formed / {tex}3 {MnO}_{4}^{2-}+4 {H}^{+} \rightarrow 2 {MnO}_{4}^{-}+{MnO}_{2}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} Potassium manganate/ {tex}{K}_{2} {MnO}_{4}{/tex} is formed/ {tex}2 {KMnO}_{4} \rightarrow {~K}_{2} {MnO}_{4}+{MnO}_{2}+{O}_{2}{/tex}
solution: Due to formation of chromate {tex}/ {CrO}_{4}{ }^{2-}{/tex} ion Due to completely filled d-orbitals in ground state as well as oxidised state. Because {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} is more stable due to stable {tex}3 {~d}^{5}{/tex} configuration whereas {tex}{Cr}^{3+}{/tex} is more stable due to stable {tex}{t}_{2 g}{ }^{3}{/tex} configuration. it changes to permanaganate ion / {tex}{MnO}_{4}{ }^{-}{/tex} is formed / {tex}3 {MnO}_{4}^{2-}+4 {H}^{+} \rightarrow 2 {MnO}_{4}^{-}+{MnO}_{2}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} Potassium manganate/ {tex}{K}_{2} {MnO}_{4}{/tex} is formed/ {tex}2 {KMnO}_{4} \rightarrow {~K}_{2} {MnO}_{4}+{MnO}_{2}+{O}_{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Due to formation of chromate {tex}/ {CrO}_{4}{ }^{2-}{/tex} ion
    2. Due to completely filled d-orbitals in ground state as well as oxidised state.
    3. Because {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} is more stable due to stable {tex}3 {~d}^{5}{/tex} configuration whereas {tex}{Cr}^{3+}{/tex} is more stable due to stable {tex}{t}_{2 g}{ }^{3}{/tex} configuration.
    1. it changes to permanaganate ion / {tex}{MnO}_{4}{ }^{-}{/tex}is formed /
      {tex}3 {MnO}_{4}^{2-}+4 {H}^{+} \rightarrow 2 {MnO}_{4}^{-}+{MnO}_{2}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex}
    2. Potassium manganate/ {tex}{K}_{2} {MnO}_{4}{/tex} is formed/
      {tex}2 {KMnO}_{4} \rightarrow {~K}_{2} {MnO}_{4}+{MnO}_{2}+{O}_{2}{/tex}

question_html:
1. While studying the periodic properties, Arti came across an abnormal behaviour in the atomic size of Hf. She found that, even though Hf is placed below Zr in the same group, both have almost similar atomic sizes.
    1. Which phenomenon is responsible for the above behaviour? Define it.
    2. Mention any other consequence of the above phenomenon.
  2. Give reasons for the following:
    1. Transition metals exhibit catalytic properties.
    2. Transition metals have high enthalpy of atomisation.
    3. Sc is a transition element, while Zn is not.
question_text: While studying the periodic properties, Arti came across an abnormal behaviour in the atomic size of Hf. She found that, even though Hf is placed below Zr in the same group, both have almost similar atomic sizes. Which phenomenon is responsible for the above behaviour? Define it. Mention any other consequence of the above phenomenon. Give reasons for the following: Transition metals exhibit catalytic properties. Transition metals have high enthalpy of atomisation. Sc is a transition element, while Zn is not.
explanation: Lanthanoid contraction. The steady decrease in atomic and ionic radii in lanthanoid series. Decrease in basic character from left to right in lanthanoid series. They have the ability to exhibit variable oxidation states/tendency to form complex compounds/ large surface area. Due to involvement of ( {tex}{n}-1{/tex} ) d and ns electrons which results in strong metallic bond and strong interatomic bonding. Sc has incompletely filled d orbital ( {tex}3 d^{1}{/tex} ) in its ground state whereas Zn has completely filled d orbital {tex}\left(3 {~d}^{10}\right){/tex} in ground state as well as in its oxidized state.
solution: Lanthanoid contraction. The steady decrease in atomic and ionic radii in lanthanoid series. Decrease in basic character from left to right in lanthanoid series. They have the ability to exhibit variable oxidation states/tendency to form complex compounds/ large surface area. Due to involvement of ( {tex}{n}-1{/tex} ) d and ns electrons which results in strong metallic bond and strong interatomic bonding. Sc has incompletely filled d orbital ( {tex}3 d^{1}{/tex} ) in its ground state whereas Zn has completely filled d orbital {tex}\left(3 {~d}^{10}\right){/tex} in ground state as well as in its oxidized state.
solution_html:
1. Lanthanoid contraction.
      The steady decrease in atomic and ionic radii in lanthanoid series.
    2. Decrease in basic character from left to right in lanthanoid series.
    1. They have the ability to exhibit variable oxidation states/tendency to form complex compounds/ large surface area.
    2. Due to involvement of ({tex}{n}-1{/tex}) d and ns electrons which results in strong metallic bond and strong interatomic bonding.
    3. Sc has incompletely filled d orbital ({tex}3 d^{1}{/tex}) in its ground state whereas Zn has completely filled d orbital {tex}\left(3 {~d}^{10}\right){/tex} in ground state as well as in its oxidized state.

The actinoid contraction is greater from element to element than lanthanoid contraction. {tex}{E}_{{Zn}^{2+} / {Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} value is more negative than expected, whereas {tex}{E}_{{Cu}^{2+} / {Cu}}^{\circ}{/tex} is positive.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html: What is Lanthanoid contraction?
question_text: What is Lanthanoid contraction?
explanation: The overall decrease in atomic and ionic radii from lanthanum to lutetium (the lanthanoid contraction) is a unique feature in the chemistry of the lanthanoids. This steady decrease in atomic and ionic size with the increase in atomic number is called Lanthanoid contraction. This causes the radii of the members of the third transition series to be very similar to those of the corresponding members of the second series.

This causes the radii of the members of the third transition series to be very similar to those of the corresponding members of the second series.

Sc 3+ , Fe 3+ , Zn 2+ [Atomic number: Sc = 21, Fe = 26, Zn = 30] How can you obtain potassium dichromate from sodium chromate? Why do transition metals and their compounds show catalytic activities?
explanation: CuCl 2 is more stable than Cu 2 Cl 2 as Cu +2 is more stable than Cu + due to high {tex}\Delta{/tex} hydH 0 /Cu + in aqueous solution undergoes disproportionation, i.e., 2Cu + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu 2+ (aq) + Cu(s) (n - 2)f 1-14 (n - 1)d o-1 ns 2 Fe 3+ , presence of unpaired electron leading to d-d transition. 2Na 2 CrO 4 + 2 H + {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Na 2 Cr 2 O 7 + 2 Na + + H 2 O Na 2 Cr 2 O 7 + 2 KCI {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} K 2 Cr 2 O 7 + 2 NaCl Because of their ability to show variable oxidation states and complex formation / provide large surface area.
solution: CuCl 2 is more stable than Cu 2 Cl 2 as Cu +2 is more stable than Cu + due to high {tex}\Delta{/tex} hydH 0 /Cu + in aqueous solution undergoes disproportionation, i.e., 2Cu + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu 2+ (aq) + Cu(s) (n - 2)f 1-14 (n - 1)d o-1 ns 2 Fe 3+ , presence of unpaired electron leading to d-d transition. 2Na 2 CrO 4 + 2 H + {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Na 2 Cr 2 O 7 + 2 Na + + H 2 O Na 2 Cr 2 O 7 + 2 KCI {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} K 2 Cr 2 O 7 + 2 NaCl Because of their ability to show variable oxidation states and complex formation / provide large surface area.

question_html:
1. Account for the following:
    1. The melting and boiling points of Zn, Cd and Hg are low.
    2. Of the d4 species, Cr2+ is strongly reducing while Mn3+ is strongly oxidizing.
    3. Eo value of Cu2+/Cu is + 0.34 V.
  2. Complete and balance the following chemical equations:
    1. KMnO4 {tex}\xrightarrow{\mathrm{heat}}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+6 \mathrm{I}^{-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}
question_text: Account for the following: The melting and boiling points of Zn, Cd and Hg are low. Of the d 4 species, Cr 2+ is strongly reducing while Mn 3+ is strongly oxidizing. E o value of Cu 2+ /Cu is + 0.34 V. Complete and balance the following chemical equations: KMnO 4 {tex}\xrightarrow{\mathrm{heat}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+6 \mathrm{I}^{-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}
explanation: Because of the absence of unpaired electrons in their d-orbitals resulting in weak bonding between the atoms/due to presence of fully filled d orbitals, weak metallic bonding takes place. Because Cr is more stable in +3 due to stable {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^3{/tex} configuration while Mn is more stable in +2 due to stable d 5 configuration. Because of high {tex}\Delta \mathrm{a} \mathrm{H}^0{/tex} and low {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}{/tex} , E o value for Cu is positive. 2KMnO 4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} K 2 MnO 4 + MnO 2 + O 2 {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7{ }^{2-}+6 \mathrm{I}^{-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+} {/tex} {tex}\rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{I}_2+7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}{/tex}
solution: Because of the absence of unpaired electrons in their d-orbitals resulting in weak bonding between the atoms/due to presence of fully filled d orbitals, weak metallic bonding takes place. Because Cr is more stable in +3 due to stable {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^3{/tex} configuration while Mn is more stable in +2 due to stable d 5 configuration. Because of high {tex}\Delta \mathrm{a} \mathrm{H}^0{/tex} and low {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}{/tex} , E o value for Cu is positive. 2KMnO 4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} K 2 MnO 4 + MnO 2 + O 2 {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7{ }^{2-}+6 \mathrm{I}^{-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+} {/tex} {tex}\rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{I}_2+7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Because of the absence of unpaired electrons in their d-orbitals resulting in weak bonding between the atoms/due to presence of fully filled d orbitals, weak metallic bonding takes place.
    2. Because Cr is more stable in +3 due to stable {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^3{/tex} configuration while Mn is more stable in +2 due to stable d5 configuration.
    3. Because of high {tex}\Delta \mathrm{a} \mathrm{H}^0{/tex} and low {tex}\Delta_{\text {hyd }} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}{/tex}, Eo value for Cu is positive.
    1. 2KMnO4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} K2MnO4 + MnO2 + O2
    2. {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7{ }^{2-}+6 \mathrm{I}^{-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+} {/tex} {tex}\rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{I}_2+7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Transition metals have incomplete d-subshell either in neutral atom or in their ions. The presence of partly filled d-orbitals in their atoms makes transition elements different from that of the non-transition elements. With partly filled d-orbitals, these elements exhibit certain characteristic properties such as display of a variety of oxidation states, formation of coloured ions and entering into complex formation with a variety of ligands. The transition metals and their compounds also exhibit catalytic properties and paramagnetic behaviour. The transition metals are very hard and have low volatility. An examination of the {tex}\text E^\text o_{\frac {\text M^{2+} }{\text M}}{/tex} values shows the varying trends:

{tex}\text E^\text o_{\frac {\text M^{2+} }{\text M}}{/tex}
V -1.18
Cr -0.91
Mn -1.18
Fe -0.44
Co -0.28
Ni -0.25
Cu +0.34
Zn -0.76

Answer the following questions:

  1.  On what basis can we say that Cu is a transition element but Zn is not? (Atomic number: Cu = 29, Zn = 30) (1)
  2. Why do transition elements show variety of oxidation states? (1)
    1. Why do {tex}\text E^\text o_{\frac {\text M^{2+} }{\text M}}{/tex} values show irregular trend from Vanadium to Zinc?
    2. How is the variability in oxidation states of transition metals different from that of the non-transition elements? (2{tex}\times{/tex}1 = 2)

      OR

    1. Of the d4 species, Cr2+ is strongly reducing while Mn3+ is strongly oxidizing. Why? (Atomic number: Cr = 24, Mn = 25)
    2. Complete the following ionic equation: (2{tex}\times{/tex}1 = 2)
      {tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{I}^{-} \longrightarrow{/tex}
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Transition metals have incomplete d-subshell either in neutral atom or in their ions. The presence of partly filled d-orbitals in their atoms makes transition elements different from that of the non-transition elements. With partly filled d-orbitals, these elements exhibit certain characteristic properties such as display of a variety of oxidation states, formation of coloured ions and entering into complex formation with a variety of ligands. The transition metals and their compounds also exhibit catalytic properties and paramagnetic behaviour. The transition metals are very hard and have low volatility. An examination of the {tex}\text E^\text o_{\frac {\text M^{2+} }{\text M}}{/tex} values shows the varying trends: {tex}\text E^\text o_{\frac {\text M^{2+} }{\text M}}{/tex} V -1.18 Cr -0.91 Mn -1.18 Fe -0.44 Co -0.28 Ni -0.25 Cu +0.34 Zn -0.76 Answer the following questions: On what basis can we say that Cu is a transition element but Zn is not? (Atomic number: Cu = 29, Zn = 30) (1) Why do transition elements show variety of oxidation states? (1) Why do {tex}\text E^\text o_{\frac {\text M^{2+} }{\text M}}{/tex} values show irregular trend from Vanadium to Zinc? How is the variability in oxidation states of transition metals different from that of the non-transition elements? (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 2) OR Of the d 4 species, Cr 2+ is strongly reducing while Mn 3+ is strongly oxidizing. Why? (Atomic number: Cr = 24, Mn = 25) Complete the following ionic equation: (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 2) {tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{I}^{-} \longrightarrow{/tex}
explanation: Cu has incomplete d-orbital in +2 oxidation state whereas Zn has fully filled d-orbital in ground state as well as in +2 oxidation state. Because both (n-1)d and ns subshell electrons take part in the bond formation due to their comparable energies/ due to the presence of unpaired electrons in d-orbitals. Because of irregular values of {tex}(\triangle_\text i\text H_1+\triangle_\text i\text H_2){/tex} and sublimation enthalpies. In transition metals, oxidation states differ by +1 whereas in non-transition metals differ by +2. OR Because Cr 2+ will be converted to Cr 3+ which has more stable half filled t 2g configuration while Mn 3+ changes to Mn 2+ which has more stable half-filled d 5 configuration. {tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{I}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 MnO 2 + 2 OH - + IO 3 -
solution: Cu has incomplete d-orbital in +2 oxidation state whereas Zn has fully filled d-orbital in ground state as well as in +2 oxidation state. Because both (n-1)d and ns subshell electrons take part in the bond formation due to their comparable energies/ due to the presence of unpaired electrons in d-orbitals. Because of irregular values of {tex}(\triangle_\text i\text H_1+\triangle_\text i\text H_2){/tex} and sublimation enthalpies. In transition metals, oxidation states differ by +1 whereas in non-transition metals differ by +2. OR Because Cr 2+ will be converted to Cr 3+ which has more stable half filled t 2g configuration while Mn 3+ changes to Mn 2+ which has more stable half-filled d 5 configuration. {tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{I}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 MnO 2 + 2 OH - + IO 3 -
solution_html:
1. Cu has incomplete d-orbital in +2 oxidation state whereas Zn has fully filled d-orbital in ground state as well as in +2 oxidation state.
  2. Because both (n-1)d and ns subshell electrons take part in the bond formation due to their comparable energies/ due to the presence of unpaired electrons in d-orbitals.
    1. Because of irregular values of {tex}(\triangle_\text i\text H_1+\triangle_\text i\text H_2){/tex} and sublimation enthalpies.
    2. In transition metals, oxidation states differ by +1 whereas in non-transition metals differ by +2.

      OR

    1. Because Cr2+ will be converted to Cr3+ which has more stable half filled t2g configuration while Mn3+ changes to Mn2+ which has more stable half-filled d5 configuration.
    2. {tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{I}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 MnO2 + 2 OH- + IO3-

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
The involvement of (n - 1)d electrons in the behaviour of transition elements impart certain distinct characteristics to these elements. Thus, in addition to variable oxidation states, they exhibit paramagnetic behaviour, catalytic properties and tendency for the formation of coloured ions. The transition metals react with a number of non-metals like oxygen, nitrogen and halogens. KMnO4 and K2Cr2O7 are common examples.
The two series of inner transition elements, lanthanoids and actinoids, constitute the f-block of the periodic table. In the lanthanoids, there is regular decrease in atomic size with increase in atomic number due to the imperfect shielding effect of 4f-orbital electrons which causes contraction.
Answer the following questions:

  1. Why do transition metals and their compounds act as good catalysts? (1)
  2. What is the cause of contraction in the atomic size of lanthanoids? (1)
  3. Define lanthanoid contraction. How does it affect the atomic radii of the third transition series and the second transition series? (2)

    OR

  1. In aqueous media, which is a stronger reducing agent -  Cr2+ or Fe2+ and why? (2)

The two series of inner transition elements, lanthanoids and actinoids, constitute the f-block of the periodic table. In the lanthanoids, there is regular decrease in atomic size with increase in atomic number due to the imperfect shielding effect of 4f-orbital electrons which causes contraction. Answer the following questions: Why do transition metals and their compounds act as good catalysts? (1) What is the cause of contraction in the atomic size of lanthanoids? subject: Chemistry

question_html: Why first ionisation enthalpy of chromium is lower than that of Zn?
question_text: Why first ionisation enthalpy of chromium is lower than that of Zn?
explanation: The electronic configuration of chromium and zinc are respectively: Cr(24) = [Ar] 3d 5 4s 2 Zn(30) = [Ar]3d 10 4s 2 It is easy to remove electron from 4 s 1 -orbital (unpaired) rather than from 4s 2 -orbital (paired). Therefore, first ionisation enthalpy of Cr is less than Zn.
solution: The electronic configuration of chromium and zinc are respectively: Cr(24) = [Ar] 3d 5 4s 2 Zn(30) = [Ar]3d 10 4s 2 It is easy to remove electron from 4 s 1 -orbital (unpaired) rather than from 4s 2 -orbital (paired). Therefore, first ionisation enthalpy of Cr is less than Zn.
solution_html:
The electronic configuration of chromium and zinc are respectively:
Cr(24) = [Ar] 3d54s2
Zn(30) = [Ar]3d104s2
It is easy to remove electron from 4 s1 -orbital (unpaired) rather than from 4s2 -orbital (paired). Therefore, first ionisation enthalpy of Cr is less than Zn.

question_text: Assign reason for each of the following: Manganese exhibits the highest oxidation state of +7 among the 3d series of transition elements. Transition metals and their compounds are generally found to be good catalysts in chemical reactions. Cr 2+ is reducing in nature while with the same d-orbital configuration (d 4 ) Mn 3+ is an oxidising agent. Zn has lowest enthalpy of atomization. Cu + is unstable in an aqueous solution.

Transition and their metal are good catalyst due to variable oxidation state/due to the ability to adopt multiple oxidation states/due to the large surface area/due to complex formation. Cr 2+ changes from d 4 to stable half-filled {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}^3}{/tex} configuration while Mn 3+ changes to stable half-filled d 5 configuration. Due to the absence of unpaired electrons and weak interatomic interactions. Cu + ion (aq.) undergoes disproportionation to Cu 2+ (aq.) and {tex}\frac {Cu}{ 2Cu^+(aq.)}{/tex} {tex}\to{/tex} Cu 2+ (aq.) + Cu(s)
solution: Manganese exhibits the highest oxidation state of +7 due to the participation of all 3d and 4s electrons in bond formation /due to the presence of maximum number of unpaired electrons. Due to the absence of unpaired electrons and weak interatomic interactions. 1. Manganese exhibits the highest oxidation state of +7 due to the participation of all 3d and 4s electrons in bond formation /due to the presence of maximum number of unpaired electrons.
  2. Transition and their metal are good catalyst due to variable oxidation state/due to the ability to adopt multiple oxidation states/due to the large surface area/due to complex formation.
  3. Cr2+ changes from d4 to stable half-filled {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}^3}{/tex} configuration while Mn3+ changes to stable half-filled d5 configuration.
  4. Due to the absence of unpaired electrons and weak interatomic interactions.
  5. Cu+ ion (aq.) undergoes disproportionation to Cu2+ (aq.) and {tex}\frac {Cu}{ 2Cu^+(aq.)}{/tex} {tex}\to{/tex} Cu2+(aq.) + Cu(s)

Cerium/Terbium Due to ability of oxygen to form multiple bonds with transition metals. KMnO 4 being an oxidising agent oxidises HCl to chlorine (Cl 2 ) therefore it is not useful to acidify KMnO 4 .
solution: Copper, Copper, due to high {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{sub}} \mathrm{H}{/tex} and low {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{Hyd}} \mathrm{H}{/tex} / The sum of enthalpies of sublimation and ionisation of copper is not balanced by the hydration enthalpy. Cerium/Terbium Due to ability of oxygen to form multiple bonds with transition metals. KMnO 4 being an oxidising agent oxidises HCl to chlorine (Cl 2 ) therefore it is not useful to acidify KMnO 4 .

Complete reaction is as follows {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CrO}_4^{2-}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html:
1. A transition element X has electronic configuration [Ar] 4s2 3d3. Predict its likely oxidation states.
  2. Complete the reaction mentioning all the products formed:
    2 KMnO4 {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta}{/tex}
  3. Account for the following:
    1. In the 3d transition series, zinc has the lowest enthalpy of atomisation.
    2. Cu+ ion is unstable in aqueous solution.
    3. Actinoids show more number of oxidation states than lanthanoids.
question_text: A transition element X has electronic configuration [Ar] 4s 2 3d 3 . Predict its likely oxidation states. Complete the reaction mentioning all the products formed: 2 KMnO 4 {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta}{/tex} Account for the following: In the 3d transition series, zinc has the lowest enthalpy of atomisation. Cu + ion is unstable in aqueous solution. Actinoids show more number of oxidation states than lanthanoids.
explanation: X = [Ar] 4s 2 3d 3 Vanadium, a transition element, is the one that has been provided. The variable oxidation states +2, +3, +4 and +5 will be displayed. Complete reaction - 2 KMnO 4 {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta}{/tex} K 2 MnO 4 + MnO 2 + O 2 The extent of metallic bonding an element undergoes decides the enthalpy of atomisation. The more extensive the metallic bonding of an element, the more will be its enthalpy of atomisation. In all transition metals (except Zn, electronic configuration: 3 d 10 4s 2 ), there are some unpaired electrons that account for their stronger metallic bonding. Due to the absence of these unpaired electrons, in Zn, leads to least enthalpy of atomisation. Cu 2+ is more stable than Cu + in aqueous medium. Cu 2+ has high hydration energy that compensates for the energy required to remove one electron from Cu + to form Cu 2+ . So in aqueous medium, Cu + gives Cu 2+ and Cu. Chemical reaction is as follows: {tex}2 \mathrm{Cu}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{2+}+\mathrm{Cu}_{(\mathrm{s})}{/tex} Actinides exhibit larger oxidation states than lanthanides, because of the very small energy gap between 5f, 6d and 7s subshells as compared to 4f, 5d and 6s orbitals in the case of lanthanoids. Thus, the outermost electrons get easily excited to the higher energy levels, giving variable oxidation states.
solution: X = [Ar] 4s 2 3d 3 Vanadium, a transition element, is the one that has been provided. The variable oxidation states +2, +3, +4 and +5 will be displayed. Complete reaction - 2 KMnO 4 {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta}{/tex} K 2 MnO 4 + MnO 2 + O 2 The extent of metallic bonding an element undergoes decides the enthalpy of atomisation. The more extensive the metallic bonding of an element, the more will be its enthalpy of atomisation. In all transition metals (except Zn, electronic configuration: 3 d 10 4s 2 ), there are some unpaired electrons that account for their stronger metallic bonding. Due to the absence of these unpaired electrons, in Zn, leads to least enthalpy of atomisation. Cu 2+ is more stable than Cu + in aqueous medium. Cu 2+ has high hydration energy that compensates for the energy required to remove one electron from Cu + to form Cu 2+ . So in aqueous medium, Cu + gives Cu 2+ and Cu. Chemical reaction is as follows: {tex}2 \mathrm{Cu}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{2+}+\mathrm{Cu}_{(\mathrm{s})}{/tex} Actinides exhibit larger oxidation states than lanthanides, because of the very small energy gap between 5f, 6d and 7s subshells as compared to 4f, 5d and 6s orbitals in the case of lanthanoids. Thus, the outermost electrons get easily excited to the higher energy levels, giving variable oxidation states.
solution_html:
1. X = [Ar] 4s23d3
    Vanadium, a transition element, is the one that has been provided. The variable oxidation states +2, +3, +4 and +5 will be displayed.
  2. Complete reaction - 2 KMnO4 {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta}{/tex} K2MnO4 + MnO2 + O2
    1. The extent of metallic bonding an element undergoes decides the enthalpy of atomisation. The more extensive the metallic bonding of an element, the more will be its enthalpy of atomisation. In all transition metals (except Zn, electronic configuration: 3 d10 4s2), there are some unpaired electrons that account for their stronger metallic bonding. Due to the absence of these unpaired electrons, in Zn, leads to least enthalpy of atomisation.
    2. Cu2+ is more stable than Cu+ in aqueous medium. Cu2+ has high hydration energy that compensates for the energy required to remove one electron from Cu+ to form Cu2+. So in aqueous medium, Cu+ gives Cu2+ and Cu.
      Chemical reaction is as follows: {tex}2 \mathrm{Cu}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{2+}+\mathrm{Cu}_{(\mathrm{s})}{/tex}
    3. Actinides exhibit larger oxidation states than lanthanides, because of the very small energy gap between 5f, 6d and 7s subshells as compared to 4f, 5d and 6s orbitals in the case of lanthanoids. Thus, the outermost electrons get easily excited to the higher energy levels, giving variable oxidation states.

question_text: Account for the following: Zn 2+ salts are colourless while Ni 2+ salts are coloured. Cr 2+ is a strong reducing agent. Transition metals and their compounds show catalytic activities. Write the ionic equations for the oxidizing action of {tex}\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}{/tex} in acidic medium with I - ion, and Fe 2+ ion
explanation: Because of no unpaired electron in d-orbitals in Zn 2+ whereas, Ni 2+ has 2 unpaired electrons in d-orbitals/Ni 2+ shows d-d transition while Zn 2+ does not. Cr 2+ is strong reducing agent because Cr is more stable in + 3 oxidation state due to stable {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}{ }^3{/tex} configuration. Transition metal show catalyst activity because of their ability to show multiple or variable oxidation states/ability to form complex/provide larger surface area for the reactants. {tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}{/tex} + 10 I - + 16 H + {tex}\to{/tex} 2 Mn 2+ + 5 I 2 + 8 H 2 O {tex} \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}{/tex} + 5 Fe 2+ + 8 H + {tex}\to{/tex} Mn 2+ + 5 Fe 3+ + 4 H 2 O
solution: Because of no unpaired electron in d-orbitals in Zn 2+ whereas, Ni 2+ has 2 unpaired electrons in d-orbitals/Ni 2+ shows d-d transition while Zn 2+ does not. Cr 2+ is strong reducing agent because Cr is more stable in + 3 oxidation state due to stable {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}{ }^3{/tex} configuration. Transition metal show catalyst activity because of their ability to show multiple or variable oxidation states/ability to form complex/provide larger surface area for the reactants. {tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}{/tex} + 10 I - + 16 H + {tex}\to{/tex} 2 Mn 2+ + 5 I 2 + 8 H 2 O {tex} \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}{/tex} + 5 Fe 2+ + 8 H + {tex}\to{/tex} Mn 2+ + 5 Fe 3+ + 4 H 2 O
solution_html:
1. Because of no unpaired electron in d-orbitals in Zn2+ whereas, Ni2+ has 2 unpaired electrons in d-orbitals/Ni2+ shows d-d transition while Zn2+ does not.
    2. Cr2+ is strong reducing agent because Cr is more stable in + 3 oxidation state due to stable {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}{ }^3{/tex} configuration.
    3. Transition metal show catalyst activity because of their ability to show multiple or variable oxidation states/ability to form complex/provide larger surface area for the reactants.
    1. {tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}{/tex} + 10 I- + 16 H+ {tex}\to{/tex} 2 Mn2+ + 5 I2 + 8 H2O
    2. {tex} \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}{/tex} + 5 Fe2+ + 8 H+ {tex}\to{/tex} Mn2+ + 5 Fe3+ + 4 H2O

question_text: Why is chemistry of actinoids complicated as compared to lanthanoids? Complete the following reaction and justify that it is a disproportionation reaction: {tex}3 \mathrm{MnO}_4{ }^{2-}{/tex} + 4H + {tex}\to{/tex} ________ + ________ + 2 H 2 O. The given graph shows the trends in melting points of transition metals: Explain the reason why Cr has highest melting point and manganese (Mn) a lower melting point.
explanation: Actinoids show wide range of oxidation states/most of them are radioactive elements therefore chemistry of actinoid is complicated as compare to lanthanoid. {tex}3 \mathrm{MnO}_4{ }^{2-}{/tex} + 4H + {tex}\to{/tex} + {tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_4{ }^{-}{/tex} + MnO 2 + 2 H 2 O Oxidation state of Mn increases from +6 to +7 (oxidation) and decreases from +6 to +4 (reduction)/Mn undergoes increase and decrease in oxidation state simultaneously. Due to the presence of a greater number of unpaired electrons which leads to strong interatomic metal-metal bonding whereas Mn with a stable half-filled d 5 configuration has a weaker metallic bond.
solution: Actinoids show wide range of oxidation states/most of them are radioactive elements therefore chemistry of actinoid is complicated as compare to lanthanoid. {tex}3 \mathrm{MnO}_4{ }^{2-}{/tex} + 4H + {tex}\to{/tex} + {tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_4{ }^{-}{/tex} + MnO 2 + 2 H 2 O Oxidation state of Mn increases from +6 to +7 (oxidation) and decreases from +6 to +4 (reduction)/Mn undergoes increase and decrease in oxidation state simultaneously. Due to the presence of a greater number of unpaired electrons which leads to strong interatomic metal-metal bonding whereas Mn with a stable half-filled d 5 configuration has a weaker metallic bond.
solution_html:
1. Actinoids show wide range of oxidation states/most of them are radioactive elements therefore chemistry of actinoid is complicated as compare to lanthanoid.
  2. {tex}3 \mathrm{MnO}_4{ }^{2-}{/tex} + 4H+ {tex}\to{/tex} + {tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_4{ }^{-}{/tex} + MnO2 + 2 H2O
    Oxidation state of Mn increases from +6 to +7 (oxidation) and decreases from +6 to +4 (reduction)/Mn undergoes increase and decrease in oxidation state simultaneously.
  3. Due to the presence of a greater number of unpaired electrons which leads to strong interatomic metal-metal bonding whereas Mn with a stable half-filled d5 configuration has a weaker metallic bond.

  3. Account for the following:

question_text: Write the number of unpaired electrons in Cr 3+ . (Atomic number of Cr = 24) Complete the reaction mentioning all the products formed: {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow{/tex} Account for the following: Mn 2+ is more stable than Fe 2+ towards oxidation to +3 state. Copper has exceptionally positive {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{2+}}^0{/tex} value. Eu 2+ with electronic configuration [Xe] 4f 7 6s 2 is a strong reducing agent.
explanation: The electronic configuration of Cr = [Ar]3d 5 4s 1 Cr 3+ = [Ar]3d 3 Hence there are 3 unpaired electrons in Cr 3+ . {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+3 \mathrm{~S}{/tex} Dichromate oxidises hydrogen sulphide to sulphur in an acidic medium. Mn 2+ = [Ar] 18 3d 5 . Fe 2+ = [Ar] 18 3d 6 . It is known that half-filled and fully-filled orbitals are more stable. Therefore, Mn in the +2 state has a stable d 5 configuration. Mn 2+ shows resistance to oxidation to Mn 3+ . Also, Fe 2+ has a 3d 6 configuration and by losing one electron, its attend more stable 3d 5 configuration. Therefore, Fe 2+ gets oxidised to Fe 3+ easily. Copper has a low hydration energy and a high atomization energy. As a result, the E 0 value is positive. Because it is more stable than Eu, Eu 2+ is a powerful reducing agent. After removing two electrons, the electronic configuration is 4f 7 6s 2 , resulting in a half-filled (f) electronic configuration. Therefore it acts as a strong reducing agent.
solution: The electronic configuration of Cr = [Ar]3d 5 4s 1 Cr 3+ = [Ar]3d 3 Hence there are 3 unpaired electrons in Cr 3+ . {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+3 \mathrm{~S}{/tex} Dichromate oxidises hydrogen sulphide to sulphur in an acidic medium. Mn 2+ shows resistance to oxidation to Mn 3+ . Also, Fe 2+ has a 3d 6 configuration and by losing one electron, its attend more stable 3d 5 configuration. Therefore, Fe 2+ gets oxidised to Fe 3+ easily. Copper has a low hydration energy and a high atomization energy. As a result, the E 0 value is positive. Because it is more stable than Eu, Eu 2+ is a powerful reducing agent. After removing two electrons, the electronic configuration is 4f 7 6s 2 , resulting in a half-filled (f) electronic configuration. Therefore it acts as a strong reducing agent.
solution_html:
1. The electronic configuration of
    Cr = [Ar]3d54s1
    Cr3+ = [Ar]3d3
    Hence there are 3 unpaired electrons in Cr3+.
  2. {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+3 \mathrm{~S}{/tex}
    Dichromate oxidises hydrogen sulphide to sulphur in an acidic medium.
    1.  Mn2+ = [Ar]18 3d5.
      Fe2+ = [Ar]18 3d6.
      It is known that half-filled and fully-filled orbitals are more stable. Therefore, Mn in the +2 state has a stable d5 configuration.
    2. Mn2+ shows resistance to oxidation to Mn3+. Also, Fe2+ has a 3d6 configuration and by losing one electron, its attend more stable 3d5 configuration. Therefore, Fe2+ gets oxidised to Fe3+ easily.
    3. Copper has a low hydration energy and a high atomization energy. As a result, the E0 value is positive.
    4. Because it is more stable than Eu, Eu2+ is a powerful reducing agent. After removing two electrons, the electronic configuration is 4f76s2, resulting in a half-filled (f) electronic configuration. Therefore it acts as a strong reducing agent.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html: Why do transition elements show variable oxidation states?
question_text: Why do transition elements show variable oxidation states?
explanation: ns and (n-1) d electrons of transition metal have very little difference in the energies and hence both can participate in bonding, which results in variable oxidation states. When ns electrons take part in bonding, they exhibit lower oxidation states whereas when (n -1 )d electrons along with ns electrons participate in bonding, they exhibit higher oxidation states.
solution: ns and (n-1) d electrons of transition metal have very little difference in the energies and hence both can participate in bonding, which results in variable oxidation states. When ns electrons take part in bonding, they exhibit lower oxidation states whereas when (n -1 )d electrons along with ns electrons participate in bonding, they exhibit higher oxidation states.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html: Define transition elements. Write two characteristics of transition elements.
question_text: Define transition elements. Write two characteristics of transition elements.
explanation: The elements which lie in between s-and p-block elements in the long form of periodic table belonging to groups 3-12 in which different electrons of d-orbitals are progressively filled in each of the four long periods are called transition elements. Their general electronic configuration is (n-1)d 1-10 ns 1-2 . The general characteristics of transition elements are their high melting and boiling points, paramagnetic behaviour, variable oxidation states, catalytic properties, etc.
solution: The elements which lie in between s-and p-block elements in the long form of periodic table belonging to groups 3-12 in which different electrons of d-orbitals are progressively filled in each of the four long periods are called transition elements. Their general electronic configuration is (n-1)d 1-10 ns 1-2 .   1. most stable in aqueous solution.
  2. a strong oxidizing agent.

question_text: The following ions of the 3d-transition series are given: Ti 4+ , V 3+ , Cr 3+ , Mn 3+ (Atomic number: Ti = 22, V = 23, Cr = 24, Mn = 25) Identify the ion which is most stable in aqueous solution. a strong oxidizing agent. colourless in aqueous solution. Give suitable reason in each.
explanation: Ti 4+ = 3d, V 3+ = 3d 3 , Mn 3+ = 3d 4 , Cr 3+ = 3d 3 out of these ions Cr 3+ is most stable due to crystal feild spliting concept. In Cr 3+ , t 2 g in half filled, so extra stable. Mn 3+ is strong oxidising agent because Mn 3+ can easily be converted into Mn 2+ having d 5 configuration which is a stable half-filled configuration. Ti 4+ is colourless because it is having no unpaired e - for excitation to higher energy level and it is colourless.
solution: Ti 4+ = 3d, V 3+ = 3d 3 , Mn 3+ = 3d 4 , Cr 3+ = 3d 3 out of these ions Cr 3+ is most stable due to crystal feild spliting concept. In Cr 3+ , t 2 g in half filled, so extra stable. Mn 3+ is strong oxidising agent because Mn 3+ can easily be converted into Mn 2+ having d 5 configuration which is a stable half-filled configuration. Ti 4+ is colourless because it is having no unpaired e - for excitation to higher energy level and it is colourless.
solution_html:
1. Ti4+ = 3d, V3+ = 3d3, Mn3+ = 3d4, Cr3+ = 3d3 out of these ions Cr3+ is most stable due to crystal feild spliting concept. In Cr3+, t2g in half filled, so extra stable.
  2. Mn3+ is strong oxidising agent because Mn3+ can easily be converted into Mn2+ having d5 configuration which is a stable half-filled configuration.
  3. Ti4+ is colourless because it is having no unpaired e- for excitation to higher energy level and it is colourless.

question_html: What is Lanthanoid contraction? Write two consequences of Lanthanoid contraction.
question_text: What is Lanthanoid contraction? Write two consequences of Lanthanoid contraction.
explanation: The steady decrease in the size of lanthanoid ions (M 3+ ) with the increase in atomic number is called lanthanoid contraction. Consequences of lanthanoid contraction: Separation of lanthanoids is difficult due to lanthanoid contraction because due to this contraction their ionic radii is very small. The basic strength of hydrides decreases from La(OH) 3 to Lu(OH) 3 . Due to lanthanoid contraction, the size of M 3+ ions decreases and there is increase in the covalent character in M - OH bond.
solution: The steady decrease in the size of lanthanoid ions (M 3+ ) with the increase in atomic number is called lanthanoid contraction. Consequences of lanthanoid contraction: Separation of lanthanoids is difficult due to lanthanoid contraction because due to this contraction their ionic radii is very small. The basic strength of hydrides decreases from La(OH) 3 to Lu(OH) 3 . Due to lanthanoid contraction, the size of M 3+ ions decreases and there is increase in the covalent character in M - OH bond.
solution_html:
The steady decrease in the size of lanthanoid ions (M3+) with the increase in atomic number is called lanthanoid contraction.
Consequences of lanthanoid contraction:

  1. Separation of lanthanoids is difficult due to lanthanoid contraction because due to this contraction their ionic radii is very small.
  2. The basic strength of hydrides decreases from La(OH)3 to Lu(OH)3. Due to lanthanoid contraction, the size of M3+ ions decreases and there is increase in the covalent character in M - OH bond.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html:
Account for the following:

  1. Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii.
  2. Transition metals show variable oxidation states.
  3. Zinc has lowest enthalpy of atomisation.
question_text: Account for the following: Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii. Transition metals show variable oxidation states. Zinc has lowest enthalpy of atomisation.
explanation: Due to lanthanide contraction, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii. It can be explained on the basis of shielding effect. The electrons present in inner shells, shield the outer electrons from nuclear charge, making them experience a low effective nuclear charge. The shielding effect exerted by the electrons decreases in the order s > p > d > f. The f subshell poorly shields the outer electrons from nuclear attraction, which results in more attractive pull of nucleus on outer electron. In case of post lanthanide elements like Hf, 4f subshell is filled and it is not very effective at shielding the outer shell electrons. Therefore, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii. The transition metals have their valence electrons in (n - 1)d and ns orbitals. Since, there is very little energy difference between these orbitals, both energy levels can be used for bond formation. Thus, transition elements exhibit variable oxidation states. Due to the absence of unpaired electrons in Zinc, the interatomic bonding is weak in Zinc. Thus, the enthalpy of atomization of zinc is lowest as the result of this weak metallic bonding.

The f subshell poorly shields the outer electrons from nuclear attraction, which results in more attractive pull of nucleus on outer electron. In case of post lanthanide elements like Hf, 4f subshell is filled and it is not very effective at shielding the outer shell electrons. Therefore, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii. The transition metals have their valence electrons in (n - 1)d and ns orbitals. Since, there is very little energy difference between these orbitals, both energy levels can be used for bond formation. Thus, transition elements exhibit variable oxidation states. Due to the absence of unpaired electrons in Zinc, the interatomic bonding is weak in Zinc. Thus, the enthalpy of atomization of zinc is lowest as the result of this weak metallic bonding.

In case of post lanthanide elements like Hf, 4f subshell is filled and it is not very effective at shielding the outer shell electrons. Therefore, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii.
  2. The transition metals have their valence electrons in (n - 1)d and ns orbitals. Since, there is very little energy difference between these orbitals, both energy levels can be used for bond formation. Thus, transition elements exhibit variable oxidation states.
  3. Due to the absence of unpaired electrons in Zinc, the interatomic bonding is weak in Zinc. Thus, the enthalpy of atomization of zinc is lowest as the result of this weak metallic bonding.

question_html: Why transition metals and their compounds act as good catalyst? How is the variability in oxidation states of transition metals different from that of the non-transition metals? Illustrate with examples.
question_text: Why transition metals and their compounds act as good catalyst? How is the variability in oxidation states of transition metals different from that of the non-transition metals? Illustrate with examples.
explanation: In case of transition elements, the variability in the oxidation state is due to the participation of (n-1)d orbitals and ns orbitals. The oxidation states differ by unity. Thus, V shows +2,+3,+4 and +5 oxidation states and Mn shows +2,+3,+4,+5,+6 and +7 oxidation states. On the other hand, the variable oxidation states shown by some p-block elements differ by two units. Thus, Sn shows +2 and +4 oxidation state and indium shows +1 and +3 oxidation states.

On the other hand, the variable oxidation states shown by some p-block elements differ by two units. Thus, Sn shows +2 and +4 oxidation state and indium shows +1 and +3 oxidation states.

In case of transition elements, the variability in the oxidation state is due to the participation of (n-1)d orbitals and ns orbitals.
The oxidation states differ by unity. Thus, V shows +2,+3,+4 and +5 oxidation states and Mn shows +2,+3,+4,+5,+6 and +7 oxidation states.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html:
Account for the following:

  1. Transition metals and their compounds show catalytic activities.
  2. Zn, Cd and Hg are non-transition elements.
  3. Zr and Hf are of almost identical atomic radii.
question_text: Account for the following: Transition metals and their compounds show catalytic activities. Zn, Cd and Hg are non-transition elements. Zr and Hf are of almost identical atomic radii.
explanation: The catalytic activity of transition metals and their compounds can be explained by two basic facts: Owing to their ability to show variable oxidation states and form complexes, transition metals form unstable intermediate compounds. Thus, they provide a new path with lower activation energy, (E a ) for a reaction. Transition metals also provide a suitable surface for reactions to occur. Transition elements are characterized by partially filled (n-1)d subshells. Zn, Cd and Hg are not regarded as transition elements as they have completely filled (n-1)d subshell. The electronic configuration of zinc is [Ar]3d 10 4s 2 . The electronic configuration of cadmium is [Kr]4d 10 5s 2 . The electronic configuration of mercury is [Xe]5d 10 6s 2 . Due to lanthanide contraction, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii. It can be explained on the basis of shielding effect. The electrons present in inner shells, shield the outer electrons from nuclear charge, making them experience a low effective nuclear charge. The shielding effect exerted by the electrons decreases in the order s > p > d > f. The f subshell poorly shields the outer electrons from nuclear attraction, which results in more attractive pull of nucleus on outer electron. In case of post lanthanide elements like Hf, 4f subshell is filled and it is not very effective at shielding the outer shell electrons. Therefore, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii.

1. The catalytic activity of transition metals and their compounds can be explained by two basic facts:
    1. Owing to their ability to show variable oxidation states and form complexes, transition metals form unstable intermediate compounds. Thus, they provide a new path with lower activation energy, (Ea) for a reaction.
    2. Transition metals also provide a suitable surface for reactions to occur.
  2. Transition elements are characterized by partially filled (n-1)d subshells.
    Zn, Cd and Hg are not regarded as transition elements as they have completely filled (n-1)d subshell.
    The electronic configuration of zinc is [Ar]3d104s2.
    The electronic configuration of cadmium is [Kr]4d105s2.
    The electronic configuration of mercury is [Xe]5d106s2.
  3. Due to lanthanide contraction, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii. It can be explained on the basis of shielding effect. The electrons present in inner shells, shield the outer electrons from nuclear charge, making them experience a low effective nuclear charge. The shielding effect exerted by the electrons decreases in the order s > p > d > f. The f subshell poorly shields the outer electrons from nuclear attraction, which results in more attractive pull of nucleus on outer electron. In case of post lanthanide elements like Hf, 4f subshell is filled and it is not very effective at shielding the outer shell electrons. Therefore, Zr and Hf have almost similar atomic radii.

question_html: Define transition elements. Which of the d-block elements may not be regarded as the transition elements? Why transition metals generally form coloured compounds?
question_text: Define transition elements. Which of the d-block elements may not be regarded as the transition elements? Why transition metals generally form coloured compounds?
explanation: Transition elements are defined as an element having a d-subshell that is partially filled with electrons, or an element that has the ability to form stable cations with an incompletely filled d-orbital. e.g., Cr has, electronic configuration [Ar]3d 5 4s 1 , partially filled d-subshell, so it is transition element. Zn, Cd and Hg cannot be classified as transition elements as they have completely filled d-subshell, e.g., Zn has, electronic configuration [Ar]3d 10 4 2 , has completely filled d-subshell, so cannot be classified as transition element. Transition elements generally form coloured compounds on account of d-d transitions, due to partial absorption of visible light occur and d-d transition of electron occurs in d-orbital, which causes the colour of the compound.
solution: Transition elements are defined as an element having a d-subshell that is partially filled with electrons, or an element that has the ability to form stable cations with an incompletely filled d-orbital. e.g., Cr has, electronic configuration [Ar]3d 5 4s 1 , partially filled d-subshell, so it is transition element. Zn, Cd and Hg cannot be classified as transition elements as they have completely filled d-subshell, e.g., Zn has, electronic configuration [Ar]3d 10 4 2 , has completely filled d-subshell, so cannot be classified as transition element. Transition elements generally form coloured compounds on account of d-d transitions, due to partial absorption of visible light occur and d-d transition of electron occurs in d-orbital, which causes the colour of the compound.

1. Transition elements are defined as an element having a d-subshell that is partially filled with electrons, or an element that has the ability to form stable cations with an incompletely filled d-orbital.
    e.g., Cr has, electronic configuration [Ar]3d54s1, partially filled d-subshell, so it is transition element.
  2. Zn, Cd and Hg cannot be classified as transition elements as they have completely filled d-subshell, e.g., Zn has, electronic configuration [Ar]3d1042, has completely filled d-subshell, so cannot be classified as transition element.
  3. Transition elements generally form coloured compounds on account of d-d transitions, due to partial absorption of visible light occur and d-d transition of electron occurs in d-orbital, which causes the colour of the compound.

question_html:
Following ions of 3d transition series are given:
V3+, Cr2+, Cu+, Fe2+,
(Atomic number : V = 23, Cr = 24, Cu = 29, Fe = 26)
Identify the ion which is

  1. unstable in aqueous solution.
  2. a strong reducing agent in aqueous solution.
  3. colourless in aqueous solution?

Give suitable reason in each.
question_text: Following ions of 3d transition series are given: V 3+ , Cr 2+ , Cu + , Fe 2+ , (Atomic number : V = 23, Cr = 24, Cu = 29, Fe = 26) Identify the ion which is unstable in aqueous solution. a strong reducing agent in aqueous solution. colourless in aqueous solution? Give suitable reason in each.
explanation: In aqueous solution, V 3+ can undergo redox reactions and exhibit various oxidation states, depending on the conditions and the substances it interacts with. This inherent instability and reactivity make V 3+ relatively less stable in aqueous solution compared to the other provided ions Cr 2+ , Cu + and Fe 2+ ), which have more stable electron configurations and oxidation states. A strong reducing agent is one that readily loses electrons, and in this case, the Cu + ion has the lowest positive charge (+1) among the given ions. The lower the positive charge on the ion, the more easily it can lose an electron, making it a stronger reducing agent. Cu + readily loses an electron to form Cu 2+ . Among the provided ions from the 3d transition series (V 3+ , Cr 2+ , Cu + , and Fe 2+ ), Cr 2+ (Chromium ion) is the one that is generally colorless in aqueous solution. The color of transition metal ions in aqueous solution is often due to the presence of unpaired electrons in the partially filled 3d orbitals. In the case of Cr 2+ , it has an elect configuration of Argon (Ar) with 18 electrons, which means that all of its 3d orbitals are completely filled. As a result, Cr 2+ is typically colorless in aqueous solution because there are no unpaired electrons in its 3d orbitals to absorb visible light and give rise to color.

  2. A strong reducing agent is one that readily loses electrons, and in this case, the Cu+ ion has the lowest positive charge (+1) among the given ions. The lower the positive charge on the ion, the more easily it can lose an electron, making it a stronger reducing agent. Cu+ readily loses an electron to form Cu2+.

question_html:
Account for the following:

  1. Transition metals and their compounds act as good catalysts.
  2. Eu2+ is a strong reducing agent.
  3. Chromium is hard whereas Zinc is soft metal.
question_text: Account for the following: Transition metals and their compounds act as good catalysts. Eu 2+ is a strong reducing agent. Chromium is hard whereas Zinc is soft metal.
explanation: the unique electronic and structural properties of transition metals and their compounds make them effective catalysts by providing pathways for reactant activation, facilitating electron transfer, and participating in catalytic cycles. Europium (Eu) has an atomic number of 63, and its ground-state electron configuration is [Xe] 4f 7 6s 2 . In the Eu 2+ ion, two electrons are removed from the outermost 6s 2 orbital. As a result, the electron configuration of Eu 2+ becomes [Xe] 4f 7 , which means it has seven unpaired electrons in its 4f orbitals. The presence of these unpaired electrons makes Eu 2+ highly reducing. Chromium are having high enthalpy of atomization due to the presence of unpaired electrons, which accounts for their hardness. However, Zinc has low enthalpy of atomization as it has no unpaired electron. Hence zinc is comparatively a soft metal.
