question_text: How would you account for the following? Actinoid contraction is greater than lanthanoid contraction. Transition metals form coloured compounds. Complete the following equation: {tex}2MnO_4^ - + 6{H^ + } + 5NO_2^ - \to {/tex}
explanation: In actinoids, the 5f orbitals are filled which have more poor shielding effect than 4f orbitals in lanthanoids. Thus, the effective nuclear charge experienced by electrons in valence shells in case of actinoids is much more that that experienced by lanthanoids. Hence, the size contraction in actinoids is greater as compared to that in lanthanoids. Most of the complexes of transition elements are coloured. This is because of the absorption of radiation from visible light region to promote an electron from one of the d-orbital to another. {tex}2MnO_4^ - + 5NO_2^ - + 6{H^ + } \to 2M{n^{2 + }} + 5NO_3^ - + 3{H_2}O{/tex}
solution: In actinoids, the 5f orbitals are filled which have more poor shielding effect than 4f orbitals in lanthanoids. Thus, the effective nuclear charge experienced by electrons in valence shells in case of actinoids is much more that that experienced by lanthanoids. Hence, the size contraction in actinoids is greater as compared to that in lanthanoids. Most of the complexes of transition elements are coloured. This is because of the absorption of radiation from visible light region to promote an electron from one of the d-orbital to another. {tex}2MnO_4^ - + 5NO_2^ - + 6{H^ + } \to 2M{n^{2 + }} + 5NO_3^ - + 3{H_2}O{/tex}
solution_html:
1. In actinoids, the 5f orbitals are filled which have more poor shielding effect than 4f orbitals in lanthanoids. Thus, the effective nuclear charge experienced by electrons in valence shells in case of actinoids is much more that that experienced by lanthanoids. Hence, the size contraction in actinoids is greater as compared to that in lanthanoids.
    2. Most of the complexes of transition elements are coloured. This is because of the absorption of radiation from visible light region to promote an electron from one of the d-orbital to another.
  2. {tex}2MnO_4^ - + 5NO_2^ - + 6{H^ + } \to 2M{n^{2 + }} + 5NO_3^ - + 3{H_2}O{/tex}

question_html: Why do transition elements show variable oxidation states? In 3d series (Sc to Zn), which element shows the maximum number of oxidation states and why?
question_text: Why do transition elements show variable oxidation states? In 3d series (Sc to Zn), which element shows the maximum number of oxidation states and why?
explanation: ns and (n-1) d electrons of transition metal have very little difference in the energies and hence both can participate in bonding, which results in variable oxidation states. When ns electrons take part in bonding, they exhibit lower oxidation states whereas when (n -1)d electrons along with ns electrons participate in bonding, they exhibit higher oxidation states. Manganese exhibits all the oxidation states from +2 to +7. It shows largest number of oxidation states as it has the maximum number of unpaired electrons.
solution: ns and (n-1) d electrons of transition metal have very little difference in the energies and hence both can participate in bonding, which results in variable oxidation states. When ns electrons take part in bonding, they exhibit lower oxidation states whereas when (n -1)d electrons along with ns electrons participate in bonding, they exhibit higher oxidation states. Manganese exhibits all the oxidation states from +2 to +7. It shows largest number of oxidation states as it has the maximum number of unpaired electrons.
solution_html:
ns and (n-1) d electrons of transition metal have very little difference in the energies and hence both can participate in bonding, which results in variable oxidation states. When ns electrons take part in bonding, they exhibit lower oxidation states whereas when (n -1)d electrons along with ns electrons participate in bonding, they exhibit higher oxidation states.
Manganese exhibits all the oxidation states from +2 to +7. It shows largest number of oxidation states as it has the maximum number of unpaired electrons.

  2. Transition metals show variable oxidation states.

question_text: Give reasons. Mn shows the highest oxidation state of +7 with oxygen but with fluorine, it shows the highest oxidation state of +4. Transition metals show variable oxidation states. Actinoids show irregularities in their electronic configurations.
explanation: Highest oxide of Mn is MnO 7 because oxygen can form multiple bonds whereas fluorine can only form single bonds with metals therefore, highest fluoride of Mn is MnF 4 . ns and (n-1) d electrons of transition metal have very little difference in the energies and hence both can participate in bonding, which results in variable oxidation states. When ns electrons take part in bonding, they exhibit lower oxidation states whereas when (n -1)d electrons along with ns electrons participate in bonding, they exhibit higher oxidation states. The irregularities in the electronic configuration of the actinoids is due to extra stability of the f 0 , f 7 and f 14 occupancies of the 5f-orbitals.

When ns electrons take part in bonding, they exhibit lower oxidation states whereas when (n -1)d electrons along with ns electrons participate in bonding, they exhibit higher oxidation states. The irregularities in the electronic configuration of the actinoids is due to extra stability of the f 0 , f 7 and f 14 occupancies of the 5f-orbitals.
solution_html:
1. Highest oxide of Mn is MnO7 because oxygen can form multiple bonds whereas fluorine can only form single bonds with metals therefore, highest fluoride of Mn is MnF4.
  2. ns and (n-1) d electrons of transition metal have very little difference in the energies and hence both can participate in bonding, which results in variable oxidation states. When ns electrons take part in bonding, they exhibit lower oxidation states whereas when (n -1)d electrons along with ns electrons participate in bonding, they exhibit higher oxidation states.
  3. The irregularities in the electronic configuration of the actinoids is due to extra stability of the f0, f7 and f14 occupancies of the 5f-orbitals.

question_text: How would you account for the following? Highest fluoride of Mn is MnF 4 whereas the highest oxide is Mn 2 O 7 . Transition metals and their compounds show catalytic properties. Complete the following equation: {tex}3 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} + 4H + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}
explanation: Highest oxide of Mn is Mn 2 O 7 because oxygen can form multiple bonds whereas fluorine can only form single bonds with metals. therefore, highest fluoride of Mn is MnF 4 . Transition metals are good catalysts because of their ability to adopt multiple oxidation states and to form complexes. Transition metals because of their variable valencies and vacant d-orbitals form unstable intermediate compounds and provide a new path with the lower activation energy for the reaction. {tex}3MnO_4^{2 - } + 4{H^ + } \to 2MnO_4^ - + Mn{O_2} + 2{H_2}O{/tex}
solution: Highest oxide of Mn is Mn 2 O 7 because oxygen can form multiple bonds whereas fluorine can only form single bonds with metals. therefore, highest fluoride of Mn is MnF 4 . Transition metals are good catalysts because of their ability to adopt multiple oxidation states and to form complexes. Transition metals because of their variable valencies and vacant d-orbitals form unstable intermediate compounds and provide a new path with the lower activation energy for the reaction. {tex}3MnO_4^{2 - } + 4{H^ + } \to 2MnO_4^ - + Mn{O_2} + 2{H_2}O{/tex}

1. Highest oxide of Mn is Mn2O7 because oxygen can form multiple bonds whereas fluorine can only form single bonds with metals. therefore, highest fluoride of Mn is MnF4.

Transition metals because of their variable valencies and vacant d-orbitals form unstable intermediate compounds and provide a new path with the lower activation energy for the reaction.

question_html:
1. Account for the following:
    1. Cu+ is unstable in an aqueous solution.
    2. Transition metals form complex compounds.
  2. Complete the following equation:
    {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}{/tex} + 8H+ + {tex}3 \mathrm{NO}_{2}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}
question_text: Account for the following: Cu + is unstable in an aqueous solution. Transition metals form complex compounds. Complete the following equation: {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}{/tex} + 8H + + {tex}3 \mathrm{NO}_{2}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}
explanation: Copper (I) ions are unstable in aqueous solution and undergo disproportionation. 2Cu + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu 2+ + Cu The stability of Cu 2+ (aq) rather than Cu + (aq) is due to the much more negative {tex}\Delta_{hyd}{/tex} H of Cu 2+ (aq) than Cu + , which compensates more for the second ionization enthalpy of Cu. Transition metals form complexes due to the small size of the metal ion, high charge density, and availability of empty d-orbitals. {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}{/tex} oxidises nitrites, ( {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{-}{/tex} ) to nitrates ( {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}{/tex} ) in acidic medium. {tex}C{r_2}O_7^{2 - } + 8{H^ + } + 3NO_2^ - \to 2C{r^{3 + }} + 3NO_3^ - + 4{H_2}O{/tex}
solution: Copper (I) ions are unstable in aqueous solution and undergo disproportionation. 2Cu + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu 2+ + Cu The stability of Cu 2+ (aq) rather than Cu + (aq) is due to the much more negative {tex}\Delta_{hyd}{/tex} H of Cu 2+ (aq) than Cu + , which compensates more for the second ionization enthalpy of Cu. Transition metals form complexes due to the small size of the metal ion, high charge density, and availability of empty d-orbitals. {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}{/tex} oxidises nitrites, ( {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{-}{/tex} ) to nitrates ( {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}{/tex} ) in acidic medium. {tex}C{r_2}O_7^{2 - } + 8{H^ + } + 3NO_2^ - \to 2C{r^{3 + }} + 3NO_3^ - + 4{H_2}O{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Copper (I) ions are unstable in aqueous solution and undergo disproportionation.
      2Cu+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu2+ + Cu
      The stability of Cu2+(aq) rather than Cu+(aq) is due to the much more negative {tex}\Delta_{hyd}{/tex} H of Cu2+(aq) than Cu+, which compensates more for the second ionization enthalpy of Cu.
    2. Transition metals form complexes due to the small size of the metal ion, high charge density, and availability of empty d-orbitals.
  2. {tex}\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}{/tex} oxidises nitrites, ({tex}\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{-}{/tex} ) to nitrates ({tex}\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}{/tex}) in acidic medium.
    {tex}C{r_2}O_7^{2 - } + 8{H^ + } + 3NO_2^ - \to 2C{r^{3 + }} + 3NO_3^ - + 4{H_2}O{/tex}

question_html:
From the given data of Eo values, answer the following questions:

{tex}E_{\left(M^{2+} / M\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} Cr Mn Fe Co Ni Cu
-0.91 -1.18 -0.44 -0.28 -0.25 +0.34
  1. Why is {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}{/tex} value exceptionally positive?
  2. Why is {tex}E_{\left(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Mn}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} value highly negative as compared to other elements?
  3. Which is a stronger reducing agent Cr2+ or Fe2+? Give reason.
question_text: From the given data of E o values, answer the following questions: {tex}E_{\left(M^{2+} / M\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} Cr Mn Fe Co Ni Cu -0.91 -1.18 -0.44 -0.28 -0.25 +0.34 Why is {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}{/tex} value exceptionally positive? Why is {tex}E_{\left(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Mn}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} value highly negative as compared to other elements? Which is a stronger reducing agent Cr 2+ or Fe 2+ ? Give reason.
explanation: From the given data of E o values: E ∘ (M 2+ /M) Cr Mn Fe Co Ni Cu -0.91 -1.18 -0.44 -0.28 -0.25 +0.34 The enthalpy of atomisation is very high for Copper (Cu) but its hydration energy is very low. The high energy to transform Cu(s) to Cu 2+ (aq) is not balanced by its hydration enthalpy. Thus, for this conversion, E o value is positive. The electronic configuration of Mn is [Ar]3d 5 4s 2 . Ionisation enthalpy is lower than hydration enthalphy due to stable 3d 5 configuration. Thus, {tex}E_{M n^{2+} / M n}^{\circ}{/tex} is more negative. Cr 2+ is stronger reducing agent than Fe 2+ because d 4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} d 3 transition occurs in the case of Fe 2+ to Fe 3+ . In a medium like water, d 3 system is more stable as compared to d 5 system.
solution: From the given data of E o values: E ∘ (M 2+ /M) Cr Mn Fe Co Ni Cu -0.91 -1.18 -0.44 -0.28 -0.25 +0.34 The enthalpy of atomisation is very high for Copper (Cu) but its hydration energy is very low. The high energy to transform Cu(s) to Cu 2+ (aq) is not balanced by its hydration enthalpy. Thus, for this conversion, E o value is positive. The electronic configuration of Mn is [Ar]3d 5 4s 2 . Ionisation enthalpy is lower than hydration enthalphy due to stable 3d 5 configuration. Thus, {tex}E_{M n^{2+} / M n}^{\circ}{/tex} is more negative. Cr 2+ is stronger reducing agent than Fe 2+ because d 4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} d 3 transition occurs in the case of Fe 2+ to Fe 3+ . In a medium like water, d 3 system is more stable as compared to d 5 system.
solution_html:
From the given data of Eo values:

E∘(M2+/M) Cr Mn Fe Co Ni Cu
-0.91 -1.18 -0.44 -0.28 -0.25 +0.34
  1. The enthalpy of atomisation is very high for Copper (Cu) but its hydration energy is very low. The high energy to transform Cu(s) to Cu2+(aq) is not balanced by its hydration enthalpy. Thus, for this conversion, Eo value is positive.
  2. The electronic configuration of Mn is [Ar]3d54s2. Ionisation enthalpy is lower than hydration enthalphy due to stable 3d5 configuration. Thus, {tex}E_{M n^{2+} / M n}^{\circ}{/tex} is more negative.
  3. Cr2+ is stronger reducing agent than Fe2+ because d4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} d3 transition occurs in the case of Fe2+ to Fe3+. In a medium like water, d3 system is more stable as compared to d5 system.

question_html:
1. Account for the following:
    1. Mn shows the highest oxidation state of +7 with oxygen but with fluorine, it shows the highest oxidation state of +4.
    2. Cr2+ is a strong reducing agent.
    3. Cu2+ salts are coloured while Zn2+ salts are white.
  2. Complete the following equations:
    1. 2MnO2 + 4KOH + O2 {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta }{/tex}
    2. {tex}Cr_2O_7^{2-}{/tex} + 14H+ + 6I- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}
question_text: Account for the following: Mn shows the highest oxidation state of +7 with oxygen but with fluorine, it shows the highest oxidation state of +4. Cr 2+ is a strong reducing agent. Cu 2+ salts are coloured while Zn 2+ salts are white. Complete the following equations: 2MnO 2 + 4KOH + O 2 {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta }{/tex} {tex}Cr_2O_7^{2-}{/tex} + 14H + + 6I - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}
explanation: In case of oxygen, Mn shows the highest oxidation state of +7. This is because Mn forms p {tex}\pi{/tex} –d {tex}\pi{/tex} multiple bonds using 2p orbitals of oxygen and 3d orbitals of Mn. Therefore, highest oxide of Mn is Mn 2 O 7 . Whereas, fluorine can only form single bonds with Mn. This is due non availability of 2p orbitals in F for multiple bonding. Therefore, highest fluoride of Mn is MnF 4 . Cr 2+ is a stronger reducing agent, as on oxidation it becomes Cr 3+ . In Cr 3+ , three electrons are present in the t 2g level (Crystal field splitting due to weak field ligands). Hence, the e g level is half-filled, Cr 3+ is extremely stable. Cu 2+ salts are coloured because Cu 2+ ion has 3d 9 4s 0 valence shell configuration with one unpaired electron and therefore, it is paramagnetic in nature. Hence, Cu 2+ ion undergoes d-d transition and forms coloured salts. In contrast to Cu 2+ , Zn 2+ has all paired electrons due to 3d 10 4s 0 valence shell configuration and therefore, it does not undergo d-d transition. Hence, Zn 2+ salts are white. {tex}2Mn{O_2} + 4KOH + {O_2}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {2{K_2}Mn{O_4}}\limits_{\mathop {Potassium}\limits_{manganate} } + 2{H_2}O{/tex} {tex}Cr_2O_7^{2-}{/tex} + 14H + + 6I - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Cr 3+ + 7H 2 O + 3I 2
solution: In case of oxygen, Mn shows the highest oxidation state of +7. This is because Mn forms p {tex}\pi{/tex} –d {tex}\pi{/tex} multiple bonds using 2p orbitals of oxygen and 3d orbitals of Mn. Therefore, highest oxide of Mn is Mn 2 O 7 . Whereas, fluorine can only form single bonds with Mn. This is due non availability of 2p orbitals in F for multiple bonding. Therefore, highest fluoride of Mn is MnF 4 . Cr 2+ is a stronger reducing agent, as on oxidation it becomes Cr 3+ . In Cr 3+ , three electrons are present in the t 2g level (Crystal field splitting due to weak field ligands). Hence, the e g level is half-filled, Cr 3+ is extremely stable. Cu 2+ salts are coloured because Cu 2+ ion has 3d 9 4s 0 valence shell configuration with one unpaired electron and therefore, it is paramagnetic in nature. Hence, Cu 2+ ion undergoes d-d transition and forms coloured salts. In contrast to Cu 2+ , Zn 2+ has all paired electrons due to 3d 10 4s 0 valence shell configuration and therefore, it does not undergo d-d transition. Hence, Zn 2+ salts are white. {tex}2Mn{O_2} + 4KOH + {O_2}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {2{K_2}Mn{O_4}}\limits_{\mathop {Potassium}\limits_{manganate} } + 2{H_2}O{/tex} {tex}Cr_2O_7^{2-}{/tex} + 14H + + 6I - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Cr 3+ + 7H 2 O + 3I 2
solution_html:
1. In case of oxygen, Mn shows the highest oxidation state of +7. This is because Mn forms p{tex}\pi{/tex}–d{tex}\pi{/tex} multiple bonds using 2p orbitals of oxygen and 3d orbitals of Mn. Therefore, highest oxide of Mn is Mn2O7. Whereas, fluorine can only form single bonds with Mn. This is due non availability of 2p orbitals in F for multiple bonding. Therefore, highest fluoride of Mn is MnF4.
    2. Cr2+ is a stronger reducing agent, as on oxidation it becomes Cr3+. In Cr3+, three electrons are present in the t2g level (Crystal field splitting due to weak field ligands). Hence, the eg level is half-filled, Cr3+ is extremely stable.
    3. Cu2+ salts are coloured because Cu2+ ion has 3d94s0 valence shell configuration with one unpaired electron and therefore, it is paramagnetic in nature. Hence, Cu2+ ion undergoes d-d transition and forms coloured salts. In contrast to Cu2+, Zn2+ has all paired electrons due to 3d104s0 valence shell configuration and therefore, it does not undergo d-d transition. Hence, Zn2+ salts are white.
    1. {tex}2Mn{O_2} + 4KOH + {O_2}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {2{K_2}Mn{O_4}}\limits_{\mathop {Potassium}\limits_{manganate} } + 2{H_2}O{/tex}
    2. {tex}Cr_2O_7^{2-}{/tex} + 14H+ + 6I- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Cr3+ + 7H2O + 3I2

question_html:
Suggest reasons for the following features of transition metal chemistry:

  1. The transition metals and their compounds are usually paramagnetic.
  2. The transition metals exhibit variable oxidation states.
question_text: Suggest reasons for the following features of transition metal chemistry: The transition metals and their compounds are usually paramagnetic. The transition metals exhibit variable oxidation states.
explanation: Due to presence of unpaired electrons in d-orbitals. Due to incomplete filling of d-orbitals. Due to very small energy difference between (n - 1)d and n s- orbitals.

chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html:
The elements of 3d transition series are given as: Sc, Ti, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn.
Answer the following:

  1. Write the element which shows the maximum number of oxidation states. Give reason.
  2. Which element has the highest melting point?
  3. Which element shows only +3 oxidation state?
  4. Which element is a strong oxidising agent in +3 oxidation state and why?
  5. Write the electronic configuration of Sc and Zn.
question_text: The elements of 3d transition series are given as: Sc, Ti, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn. Answer the following: Write the element which shows the maximum number of oxidation states. Give reason. Which element has the highest melting point? Which element shows only +3 oxidation state? Which element is a strong oxidising agent in +3 oxidation state and why? Write the electronic configuration of Sc and Zn.
explanation: Mn shows the highest number of oxidation state. Mn has electronic configuration [Ar] 3d 5 4s 2 . It has the maximum number of unpaired electrons in d orbitals and all the electrons in s-orbital as well as in d-orbital can take part in bond formation, therefore, it shows +7 highest oxidation state. Chromium has the highest melting point among all the given elements. Scandium shows only +3 oxidation state. In the +3 oxidation state, Mn is a strong oxidising agent because in Mn 3+ ion, Mn exists in 3d 4 configuration which is less stable and it can reduce to Mn 2+ giving a more stable 3d 5 configuration. Hence, it acts as a strong oxidising agent. The electronic configuration is as follows: Sc- [Ar]4s 2 3d 1 Zn- [Ar]4s 2 3d 10
solution: Mn shows the highest number of oxidation state. Mn has electronic configuration [Ar] 3d 5 4s 2 . It has the maximum number of unpaired electrons in d orbitals and all the electrons in s-orbital as well as in d-orbital can take part in bond formation, therefore, it shows +7 highest oxidation state. Chromium has the highest melting point among all the given elements. Scandium shows only +3 oxidation state. In the +3 oxidation state, Mn is a strong oxidising agent because in Mn 3+ ion, Mn exists in 3d 4 configuration which is less stable and it can reduce to Mn 2+ giving a more stable 3d 5 configuration. Hence, it acts as a strong oxidising agent. The electronic configuration is as follows: Sc- [Ar]4s 2 3d 1 Zn- [Ar]4s 2 3d 10
solution_html:
1. Mn shows the highest number of oxidation state. Mn has electronic configuration [Ar] 3d54s2. It has the maximum number of unpaired electrons in d orbitals and all the electrons in s-orbital as well as in d-orbital can take part in bond formation, therefore, it shows +7 highest oxidation state.
  2. Chromium has the highest melting point among all the given elements.
  3. Scandium shows only +3 oxidation state.
  4. In the +3 oxidation state, Mn is a strong oxidising agent because in Mn3+ ion, Mn exists in 3d4 configuration which is less stable and it can reduce to Mn2+ giving a more stable 3d5 configuration. Hence, it acts as a strong oxidising agent.
  5. The electronic configuration is as follows:
    Sc- [Ar]4s23d1
    Zn-  [Ar]4s23d10

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html: What is lanthanoid contraction? What are the consequences of lanthanoid contraction?
question_text: What is lanthanoid contraction? What are the consequences of lanthanoid contraction?
explanation: The decrease in atomic and ionic size with increase in atomic number in lanthanoids is called lanthanoid contraction. Its consequences are (i) It causes the radii of the members of the third transition series to be very similar to those of the corresponding members of the second series. The almost identical radii of Zr (160 pm) and Hf (159 pm), have almost similar sizes. (ii) Difficulty in separation of lanthanoids.
solution: The decrease in atomic and ionic size with increase in atomic number in lanthanoids is called lanthanoid contraction. Its consequences are (i) It causes the radii of the members of the third transition series to be very similar to those of the corresponding members of the second series. The almost identical radii of Zr (160 pm) and Hf (159 pm), have almost similar sizes. (ii) Difficulty in separation of lanthanoids.
solution_html:
The decrease in atomic and ionic size with increase in atomic number in lanthanoids is called lanthanoid contraction. Its consequences are 

(i) It causes the radii of the members of the third transition series to be very similar to those of the corresponding members of the second series. The almost identical radii of Zr (160 pm) and Hf (159 pm), have almost similar sizes.

(ii) Difficulty in separation of lanthanoids.

explanation: Transition metals have more number of unpaired electrons in their valence shells. As a result they are able to form very strong metallic bonds and hence, they are much harder than alkali metals.

  1. Transition elements exhibit paramagnetic behaviour.
  2. Co2+ is easily oxidised in the presence of a strong ligands.
question_text: Assign reason for each of the following: Transition elements exhibit paramagnetic behaviour. Co 2+ is easily oxidised in the presence of a strong ligands.
explanation: Paramagnetism arises due to the presence of unpaired electrons. When transition metal ions have unpaired electrons in d-orbitals (d 1 to d 9 ). They exhibit paramagnetic behaviour. In Co 2+ , electronic configuration is 3d 7 . There is one unpaired electron even after pairing occurs in the presence of a strong ligand. Hence, Co 2+ is oxidised to more stable Co 3+ .

In Co 2+ , electronic configuration is 3d 7 . There is one unpaired electron even after pairing occurs in the presence of a strong ligand. Hence, Co 2+ is oxidised to more stable Co 3+ .
solution_html:
1. Paramagnetism arises due to the presence of unpaired electrons. When transition metal ions have unpaired electrons in d-orbitals (d1 to d9). They exhibit paramagnetic behaviour.
  2. In Co2+, electronic configuration is 3d7. There is one unpaired electron even after pairing occurs in the presence of a strong ligand. Hence, Co2+ is oxidised to more stable Co3+.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html:
Assign a reason for each of the following observations:

  1. The transition metals (with the exception of Zn, Cd and Hg) are hard and have high melting and boiling points.
  2. The ionisation enthalpies (first and second) in the first series of the transition elements are found to vary irregularly.
question_text: Assign a reason for each of the following observations: The transition metals (with the exception of Zn, Cd and Hg) are hard and have high melting and boiling points. The ionisation enthalpies (first and second) in the first series of the transition elements are found to vary irregularly.
explanation: The transition metals (except Zn, Cd and Hg) are hard and have high melting and boiling points because transition elements display metallic properties. They show strong metallic bonding. Greater the number of valence electrons, the stronger is the resultant bonding. Ionisation enthalpy increases with an increase in nuclear charge along with each series. However, Cr has low first IE because the loss of one electron gives stable electronic configuration (3d 5 ). Zn has very high IE because electron has to be removed from 4s orbital of the stable configuration (3d 10 4s 2 ). Similarly, Cr and Cu show much higher values for second IE because the second electron has to be removed from the stable configuration of Cr + (3d 5 ) and Cu + (3d 10 ).
solution: The transition metals (except Zn, Cd and Hg) are hard and have high melting and boiling points because transition elements display metallic properties. They show strong metallic bonding. Greater the number of valence electrons, the stronger is the resultant bonding. Ionisation enthalpy increases with an increase in nuclear charge along with each series. However, Cr has low first IE because the loss of one electron gives stable electronic configuration (3d 5 ). Zn has very high IE because electron has to be removed from 4s orbital of the stable configuration (3d 10 4s 2 ). Similarly, Cr and Cu show much higher values for second IE because the second electron has to be removed from the stable configuration of Cr + (3d 5 ) and Cu + (3d 10 ).
solution_html:
1. The transition metals (except Zn, Cd and Hg) are hard and have high melting and boiling points because transition elements display metallic properties. They show strong metallic bonding. Greater the number of valence electrons, the stronger is the resultant bonding.
  2. Ionisation enthalpy increases with an increase in nuclear charge along with each series. However, Cr has low first IE because the loss of one electron gives stable electronic configuration (3d5). Zn has very high IE because electron has to be removed from 4s orbital of the stable configuration (3d10 4s2). Similarly, Cr and Cu show much higher values for second IE because the second electron has to be removed from the stable configuration of Cr+(3d5) and Cu+(3d10).

question_html:
Assign reasons for the following:

  1. Copper (I) ion is not known to exist in aqueous solutions.
  2. Both O2 and F2 stabilise high oxidation states of transition metals but the ability of oxygen to do so exceeds that of fluorine.
question_text: Assign reasons for the following: Copper (I) ion is not known to exist in aqueous solutions. Both O 2 and F 2 stabilise high oxidation states of transition metals but the ability of oxygen to do so exceeds that of fluorine.
explanation: In aqueous solution Cu + undergoes disproportionation to form a more stable Cu 2+ ion. 2Cu + (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu 2+ (aq) + Cu(s) The higher stability of Cu 2+ in aqueous solution may be attributed to its greater negative {tex}\Delta_{hyd}{/tex} H° than that of Cu + . It compensates for the second ionization enthalpy of Cu involved in the formation of Cu 2+ ions. Both O 2 and F 2 stabilize high oxidation states but the ability of oxygen to stabilise these higher oxidation states exceeds that of fluorine due to ability of oxygen to form multiple bonds with the metal atoms. e.g. Mn forms the highest fluoride as MnF 4 whereas, the highest oxide is Mn 2 O 7 . This is due to the tendency of oxygen to form multiple bonds.
solution: In aqueous solution Cu + undergoes disproportionation to form a more stable Cu 2+ ion. 2Cu + (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu 2+ (aq) + Cu(s) The higher stability of Cu 2+ in aqueous solution may be attributed to its greater negative {tex}\Delta_{hyd}{/tex} H° than that of Cu + . It compensates for the second ionization enthalpy of Cu involved in the formation of Cu 2+ ions. Both O 2 and F 2 stabilize high oxidation states but the ability of oxygen to stabilise these higher oxidation states exceeds that of fluorine due to ability of oxygen to form multiple bonds with the metal atoms. e.g. Mn forms the highest fluoride as MnF 4 whereas, the highest oxide is Mn 2 O 7 . This is due to the tendency of oxygen to form multiple bonds.
solution_html:
1. In aqueous solution Cu+ undergoes disproportionation to form a more stable Cu2+ ion.
    2Cu+(aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu2+(aq) + Cu(s)
    The higher stability of Cu2+ in aqueous solution may be attributed to its greater negative {tex}\Delta_{hyd}{/tex}H° than that of Cu+. It compensates for the second ionization enthalpy of Cu involved in the formation of Cu2+ ions.
  2. Both O2 and F2 stabilize high oxidation states but the ability of oxygen to stabilise these higher oxidation states exceeds that of fluorine due to ability of oxygen to form multiple bonds with the metal atoms. e.g. Mn forms the highest fluoride as MnF4 whereas, the highest oxide is Mn2O7. This is due to the tendency of oxygen to form multiple bonds.

question_html:
Assign reasons for the following:

  1. Transition metals and many of their compounds act as good catalysts.
  2. Transition metals generally form coloured compounds.
question_text: Assign reasons for the following: Transition metals and many of their compounds act as good catalysts. Transition metals generally form coloured compounds.
explanation: Transition metals are good catalyst because of their ability to adopt multiple oxidation states and to form complexes. Transition metals because of their variable valencies and vacant d-orbitals form unstable intermediate compounds and provide a new path with the lower activation energy for the reaction. Most of the complexes of transition elements are coloured. This is because of the absorption of radiation from visible light region to promote an electron from one of the d-orbital to another. These are known as d-d transitions and are responsible for imparting colour to the solution. The ions of transition elements absorb the radiation of a particular wavelength and the rest is reflected, imparting colour to the solution.

question_html:
Account for the following:

  1. Mn2+ is more stable than Fe2+ towards oxidation to +3 state.
  2. The enthalphy of atomisation is lowest for Zn in first series (3d) of the transition elements.
question_text: Account for the following: Mn 2+ is more stable than Fe 2+ towards oxidation to +3 state. The enthalphy of atomisation is lowest for Zn in first series (3d) of the transition elements.
explanation: Electronic configuration of Mn 2+ is 3d 5 which is half-filled and hence stable. Therefore, third ionisation enthalpy is very high, i.e. third electron cannot be lost easily.In case of Fe 2+ electronic configuration is 3d 6 . Hence, it can lose one electron easily to give stable configuration, i.e. 3d 5 . Enthalpy of atomisation depends upon the strength of bonding. In case of zinc, only metallic bonding occurs but no d-d overlapping takes place whereas in case of other metals of first transition series, both the metallic as well as covalent bonding are present. Thus, enthalpy of atomisation is lowest for zinc.

In case of zinc, only metallic bonding occurs but no d-d overlapping takes place whereas in case of other metals of first transition series, both the metallic as well as covalent bonding are present. Thus, enthalpy of atomisation is lowest for zinc.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html:
Describe the general trends in the following properties of the first series (3d) of the transition elements:

  1. Number of oxidation states exhibited.
  2. Formation of oxo metal ions.
question_text: Describe the general trends in the following properties of the first series (3d) of the transition elements: Number of oxidation states exhibited. Formation of oxo metal ions.
explanation: In 3d-series all the elements show +2 oxidation state except Sc(Sc = +3). Oxidation states first increases from Sc to Mn due to increase in number of unpaired electrons and then decreases because pairing take place. Fe and Ni show zero oxidation state in metal carbonyls. All the metals except scandium form MO oxides which are ionic. The highest oxidation number in the oxides, coincide with the group number and is attained in Sc 2 O 3 to Mn 2 O 7 . Beyond group 7, no higher oxides of iron above Fe 2 O 3 are known. Besides the oxides, the oxocations stabilise V V as {tex}VO_2^{+}{/tex} , V IV , as VO 2+ and Ti as TiO 2+ . As the oxidation number of a metal increases, ionic character decreases. In case of Mn, MnO 7 is a covalent green oil. Even CrO 3 and V 2 O 5 have low melting points. In their higher oxides, the acidic character is predominant. Thus, Mn 2 O 7 gives H 2 MnO 4 and CrO 3 gives H 2 CrO 4 and H 2 Cr 2 O 7 . V 2 O 5 is however, amphoteric though mainly acidic and it gives {tex}VO_4^{3-}{/tex} as well as {tex}VO_2^{+}{/tex} salt.
solution: In 3d-series all the elements show +2 oxidation state except Sc(Sc = +3). Oxidation states first increases from Sc to Mn due to increase in number of unpaired electrons and then decreases because pairing take place. Fe and Ni show zero oxidation state in metal carbonyls. All the metals except scandium form MO oxides which are ionic. The highest oxidation number in the oxides, coincide with the group number and is attained in Sc 2 O 3 to Mn 2 O 7 . Beyond group 7, no higher oxides of iron above Fe 2 O 3 are known. Besides the oxides, the oxocations stabilise V V as {tex}VO_2^{+}{/tex} , V IV , as VO 2+ and Ti as TiO 2+ . As the oxidation number of a metal increases, ionic character decreases. In case of Mn, MnO 7 is a covalent green oil. Even CrO 3 and V 2 O 5 have low melting points. In their higher oxides, the acidic character is predominant. Thus, Mn 2 O 7 gives H 2 MnO 4 and CrO 3 gives H 2 CrO 4 and H 2 Cr 2 O 7 . V 2 O 5 is however, amphoteric though mainly acidic and it gives {tex}VO_4^{3-}{/tex} as well as {tex}VO_2^{+}{/tex} salt.
solution_html:
1. In 3d-series all the elements show +2 oxidation state except Sc(Sc = +3). Oxidation states first increases from Sc to Mn due to increase in number of unpaired electrons and then decreases because pairing take place. Fe and Ni show zero oxidation state in metal carbonyls.
  2. All the metals except scandium form MO oxides which are ionic. The highest oxidation number in the oxides, coincide with the group number and is attained in Sc2O3 to Mn2O7. Beyond group 7, no higher oxides of iron above Fe2O3 are known.
    Besides the oxides, the oxocations stabilise VV as {tex}VO_2^{+}{/tex}, VIV, as VO2+ and Ti as TiO2+. As the oxidation number of a metal increases, ionic character decreases. In case of Mn, MnO7 is a covalent green oil. Even CrO3 and V2O5 have low melting points. In their higher oxides, the acidic character is predominant. Thus, Mn2O7 gives H2MnO4 and CrO3 gives H2CrO4 and H2Cr2O7. V2O5 is however, amphoteric though mainly acidic and it gives {tex}VO_4^{3-}{/tex} as well as {tex}VO_2^{+}{/tex} salt.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html:
Assign suitable reasons for the following:

  1. The Mn2+ compounds are more stable than Fe2+ towards oxidation to their +3 state.
  2. In the 3d series from Sc (Z = 21) to Zn (Z = 30), the enthalpy of atomisation of Zn is the lowest.
  3. Sc3+ is colourless in aqueous solution whereas Ti3+ is coloured.
question_text: Assign suitable reasons for the following: The Mn 2+ compounds are more stable than Fe 2+ towards oxidation to their +3 state. In the 3d series from Sc (Z = 21) to Zn (Z = 30), the enthalpy of atomisation of Zn is the lowest. Sc 3+ is colourless in aqueous solution whereas Ti 3+ is coloured.
explanation: Electronic configuration of Mn 2+ is [Ar] 18 3 d 5 . Electronic configuration of Fe 2+ is [Ar] 18 3 d 6 . It is known that half-filled and fully-filled orbitals are more stable. Therefore, Mn in (+2) state has a stable d 5 configuration. This is the reason Mn 2+ shows resistance to oxidation to Mn 3+ . Also, Fe 2+ has 3 d 6 configuration and by losing one electron, its configuration changes to a more stable 3 d 5 configuration. Therefore, Fe 2+ easily gets oxidized to Fe +3 oxidation state. Sc & Zn belongs to 3 rd group of periodic table. In all transition metals (except Zn, electronic configuration: 3 d 10 4 s 2 ), there are some unpaired electrons that account for their stronger metallic bonding. Enthalpy of atomisation depends upon the strength of bonding. In case of zinc, only metallic bonding occurs but no d-d overlapping takes place whereas in case of other metals of first transition series, both the metallic as well as covalent bonding are present. Due to the absence of these unpaired electrons, the inter-atomic electronic bonding is the weakest in Zn and as a result, it has the least enthalpy of atomization. The electronic configuration of Scandium is [Ar] 3d 1 4s 2 , when it forms Sc 3 + , it loses all three outer electrons so the 3d level is empty. Therefore, Sc 3+ is colourless because, d-d transition are forbidden. But in Ti 3+ due to the presence of one electron in d-orbital (d 1 ), it undergoes d-d transition within partly filled d-orbitals. and hence Ti 3+ is coloured.
solution: Electronic configuration of Mn 2+ is [Ar] 18 3 d 5 . Electronic configuration of Fe 2+ is [Ar] 18 3 d 6 . It is known that half-filled and fully-filled orbitals are more stable. Therefore, Mn in (+2) state has a stable d 5 configuration. This is the reason Mn 2+ shows resistance to oxidation to Mn 3+ . Also, Fe 2+ has 3 d 6 configuration and by losing one electron, its configuration changes to a more stable 3 d 5 configuration. Therefore, Fe 2+ easily gets oxidized to Fe +3 oxidation state. Sc & Zn belongs to 3 rd group of periodic table. In all transition metals (except Zn, electronic configuration: 3 d 10 4 s 2 ), there are some unpaired electrons that account for their stronger metallic bonding. Enthalpy of atomisation depends upon the strength of bonding. In case of zinc, only metallic bonding occurs but no d-d overlapping takes place whereas in case of other metals of first transition series, both the metallic as well as covalent bonding are present. Due to the absence of these unpaired electrons, the inter-atomic electronic bonding is the weakest in Zn and as a result, it has the least enthalpy of atomization. The electronic configuration of Scandium is [Ar] 3d 1 4s 2 , when it forms Sc 3 + , it loses all three outer electrons so the 3d level is empty. Therefore, Sc 3+ is colourless because, d-d transition are forbidden. But in Ti 3+ due to the presence of one electron in d-orbital (d 1 ), it undergoes d-d transition within partly filled d-orbitals. and hence Ti 3+ is coloured.
solution_html:
1. Electronic configuration of Mn2+ is [Ar]18 3d5.
    Electronic configuration of Fe2+ is [Ar]18 3d6.
    It is known that half-filled and fully-filled orbitals are more stable. Therefore, Mn in (+2) state has a stable d5 configuration. This is the reason Mn2+ shows resistance to oxidation to Mn3+. Also, Fe2+ has 3d6 configuration and by losing one electron, its configuration changes to a more stable 3d5 configuration. Therefore, Fe2+ easily gets oxidized to Fe+3 oxidation state.
  2. Sc & Zn belongs to 3rd group of periodic table. In all transition metals (except Zn, electronic configuration: 3d10 4s2), there are some unpaired electrons that account for their stronger metallic bonding. Enthalpy of atomisation depends upon the strength of bonding. In case of zinc, only metallic bonding occurs but no d-d overlapping takes place whereas in case of other metals of first transition series, both the metallic as well as covalent bonding are present. Due to the absence of these unpaired electrons, the inter-atomic electronic bonding is the weakest in Zn and as a result, it has the least enthalpy of atomization.
  3. The electronic configuration of Scandium is [Ar] 3d14s2 , when it forms Sc3+, it loses all three outer electrons so the 3d level is empty. Therefore, Sc3+ is colourless because, d-d transition are forbidden. But in Ti3+ due to the presence of one electron in d-orbital (d1), it undergoes d-d transition within partly filled d-orbitals. and hence Ti3+ is coloured.

question_html:
1. With reference to structural variability, chemical reactivity, write the differences between lanthanoids and actinoids.
  2. Name a member of the lanthanoid series which is well known to exhibit +4 oxidation state.
  3. Complete the following equation:
    {tex}MnO_4^-{/tex} + 8H+ + 5e- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}
  4. Out of Mn3+ and Cr3+, which is more paramagnetic and why? (Atomic number of Mn = 25, Cr = 24)
question_text: With reference to structural variability, chemical reactivity, write the differences between lanthanoids and actinoids. Name a member of the lanthanoid series which is well known to exhibit +4 oxidation state. Complete the following equation: {tex}MnO_4^-{/tex} + 8H + + 5e - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Out of Mn 3+ and Cr 3+ , which is more paramagnetic and why? (Atomic number of Mn = 25, Cr = 24)
explanation: Differences between lanthanoids and actinoids Structural variability Lanthanoids Actinoids There is small regular decrease in atomic and ionic radii of lanthanoids, known as lanthanoid contraction. There is also regular decrease in atomic and ionic radii of actinoids, known as actinoid contraction, but this contraction vary from element to element due to poor shielding effect of 5f electrons. They show great structural variability. Chemical reactivity Lanthanoids Actinoids They have less tendency towards complex formation. They have stronger tendency towards complex formation. Except promethium, they are non-radioactive They are radio active They do not form oxocations They form oxocations Their oxides and hydroxides are less basic Their oxides and hydroxides are more basic Cerium (Ce) is well known to exhibit +4 oxidation state. {tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} + 8H + + 5e - {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} Mn 2+ + 4H 2 O Outer shell electronic configuration of Mn = 3d5 4s2, so, Mn 3+ = 3d 4 Number of unpaired electron in Mn 3+ = 4 Similarly, outer shell electronic configuration of Cr = 3d5 4s1 so, Cr 3+ = 3d 3 Number of unpaired electrons in Cr 3+ = 3 As, Mn 3+ has 4 unpaired electrons, therefore Mn 3+ exhibits more paramagnetism.
solution: Differences between lanthanoids and actinoids Structural variability Lanthanoids Actinoids There is small regular decrease in atomic and ionic radii of lanthanoids, known as lanthanoid contraction. There is also regular decrease in atomic and ionic radii of actinoids, known as actinoid contraction, but this contraction vary from element to element due to poor shielding effect of 5f electrons. They show great structural variability. Chemical reactivity Lanthanoids Actinoids They have less tendency towards complex formation. They have stronger tendency towards complex formation. Except promethium, they are non-radioactive They are radio active They do not form oxocations They form oxocations Their oxides and hydroxides are less basic Their oxides and hydroxides are more basic Cerium (Ce) is well known to exhibit +4 oxidation state. {tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} + 8H + + 5e - {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} Mn 2+ + 4H 2 O Outer shell electronic configuration of Mn = 3d5 4s2, so, Mn 3+ = 3d 4 Number of unpaired electron in Mn 3+ = 4 Similarly, outer shell electronic configuration of Cr = 3d5 4s1 so, Cr 3+ = 3d 3 Number of unpaired electrons in Cr 3+ = 3 As, Mn 3+ has 4 unpaired electrons, therefore Mn 3+ exhibits more paramagnetism.
solution_html:
1. Differences between lanthanoids and actinoids
    Structural variability
    Lanthanoids Actinoids
    There is small regular decrease in atomic and ionic radii of lanthanoids, known as lanthanoid contraction. There is also regular decrease in atomic and ionic radii of actinoids, known as actinoid contraction, but this contraction vary from element to element due to poor shielding effect of 5f electrons. They show great structural variability.
     
    Chemical reactivity
    Lanthanoids Actinoids
    They have less tendency towards complex formation. They have stronger tendency towards complex formation.
    Except promethium, they are non-radioactive They are radio active
    They do not form oxocations They form oxocations
    Their oxides and hydroxides are less basic Their oxides and hydroxides are more basic
  2. Cerium (Ce) is well known to exhibit +4 oxidation state.
  3. {tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} + 8H+ + 5e- {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} Mn2+ + 4H2O
  4. Outer shell electronic configuration of Mn = 3d5 4s2, so, Mn3+ = 3d4

    Number of unpaired electron in Mn3+ = 4
    Similarly, outer shell electronic configuration of Cr = 3d5 4s1 so, Cr3+ = 3d3

    Number of unpaired electrons in Cr3+ = 3
    As, Mn3+ has 4 unpaired electrons, therefore Mn3+ exhibits more paramagnetism.

explanation: Manganese exhibits all the oxidation states from +2 to +7 as it has maximum number of unpaired electrons. The enthalpy of atomisation is very high for Cu but its hydration energy is very low. The high energy to transform Cu(s) to Cu 2+ (aq) is not balanced by its hydration enthalpy. Thus, for this conversion, E 0 value is positive. Mn3+is stronger oxidising agent because the change from Mn 3+ to Mn 2+ results in half-filled (d 5 ) configuration which has extra stability. Thus, it behaves as a strong oxidising agent. On the other hand, Cr 3+ has d 3 configuration, Le.half-filled t 2g level, so it is already stable and does not reduce to less stable Cr 2+ . In the other words, it does not behave as an oxidising agent. Europium (Eu) and Ytterbium (Yb) is well known to exhibit +2 oxidation state. {tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} + 8H - + 5e - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Mn 2+ + 4H 2 O

question_html:
Which of the following cations are coloured in aqueous solutions and why?
Sc3+, V3+, Ti4+, Mn2+
(At. no Sc = 21, V = 23, ti = 22, Mn = 25)
question_text: Which of the following cations are coloured in aqueous solutions and why? Sc 3+ , V 3+ , Ti 4+ , Mn 2+ (At. no Sc = 21, V = 23, ti = 22, Mn = 25)
explanation: Electronic configuration of the ions are Sc 3+ = [Ar]3d 0 4s 0 , V 3+ = [Ar]3d 2 4s 0 , Ti 4+ = [Ar]3d 0 4s 0 and Mn 2+ = [Ar]3d 5 4s 0 . Since, V 3+ , Mn 2+ contains partially filled d-orbitals, electrons can undergo d-d transition, hence are coloured while Ti 4+ and Sc 3+ does not have any electron. Therefore, are colourless.
solution: Electronic configuration of the ions are Sc 3+ = [Ar]3d 0 4s 0 , V 3+ = [Ar]3d 2 4s 0 , Ti 4+ = [Ar]3d 0 4s 0 and Mn 2+ = [Ar]3d 5 4s 0 . Since, V 3+ , Mn 2+ contains partially filled d-orbitals, electrons can undergo d-d transition, hence are coloured while Ti 4+ and Sc 3+ does not have any electron. Therefore, are colourless.
solution_html:
Electronic configuration of the ions are
Sc3+ = [Ar]3d04s0, V3+ = [Ar]3d24s0, Ti4+ = [Ar]3d04s0 and Mn2+ = [Ar]3d54s0.
Since, V3+, Mn2+ contains partially filled d-orbitals, electrons can undergo d-d transition, hence are coloured while Ti4+ and Sc3+ does not have any electron. Therefore, are colourless.

question_html:
How would you account for the following?

  1. Transition metals exhibit variable oxidation states.
  2. Transition metals and their compounds act as catalysts.
question_text: How would you account for the following? Transition metals exhibit variable oxidation states. Transition metals and their compounds act as catalysts.
explanation: ns and (n-1)d electrons have very small energy differences in the energies and hence both can take part in bond formation, which results in variation oxidation states. When ns electrons take part in bonding, they exhibit lower oxidation states whereas when (n -1)d electrons along with ns electrons participate in bonding, they exhibit higher oxidation states. Transition metals are good catalyst because of their ability to adopt multiple oxidation states and to form complexes. Transition metals because of their variable valencies and vacant d-orbitals form unstable intermediate compounds and provide a new path with the lower activation energy for the reaction.

  2. Transition metals are good catalyst because of their ability to adopt multiple oxidation states and to form complexes. Transition metals because of their variable valencies and vacant d-orbitals form unstable intermediate compounds and provide a new path with the lower activation energy for the reaction.

question_html:
1. Give reasons for the following:
    1. Mn3+ is a good oxidising agent.
    2. {tex}E_{M^{2+} / M}^{\circ}{/tex} values are not regular for first row transition metals (3d -series).
    3. Although F is more electronegative than 0, the highest Mn fluoride is MnF4, whereas the highest oxide is Mn2O7.
  2. Complete the following equations:
    1. {tex}2CrO_4^{2 - } + 2{H^ + } \to {/tex}
    2. {tex}KMn{O_4}\xrightarrow{{Heat}}{/tex}
question_text: Give reasons for the following: Mn 3+ is a good oxidising agent. {tex}E_{M^{2+} / M}^{\circ}{/tex} values are not regular for first row transition metals (3d -series). Although F is more electronegative than 0, the highest Mn fluoride is MnF 4 , whereas the highest oxide is Mn 2 O 7 . Complete the following equations: {tex}2CrO_4^{2 - } + 2{H^ + } \to {/tex} {tex}KMn{O_4}\xrightarrow{{Heat}}{/tex}
explanation: Mn 3+ / Mn 2+ has large positive E o value. Hence, Mn 3+ can be easily reduced to Mn 2+ . Therefore, it is a good oxidising agent. Also, Mn 2+ has half-filled electronic configuration, so it is more stable than Mn 3+ state. There is decreasing negative electrode potentials of M 2+ / M in the first transition series due to increase in the sum of IE 1 and IE 2 . It shows that in general. the stability of +2 oxidation state decreases from left to right. Exceptions are Mn and Zn in which the greater stability of +2 state for Mn is due to half-filled d-subshell (d 5 ) and that of Zn is due to completely filled d-subshell (d 10 ). Highest oxide of Mn is Mn 2 O 7 because oxygen can form multiple bonds whereas fluorine can only form single bonds with metals therefore, highest fluoride of Mn is MnF 4 . {tex}2CrO_4^{2 - } + 2{H^ + } \to C{r_2}O_7^{2 - } + {H_2}O{/tex} On strong heating, KMn04 decomposes to potassium manganate. {tex}\begin{array}{l}{2 \mathrm{KMnO}_{4} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{4}+\mathrm{MnO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}} \\ {(\text { Purple })}\end{array}{/tex}

It shows that in general. the stability of +2 oxidation state decreases from left to right. Exceptions are Mn and Zn in which the greater stability of +2 state for Mn is due to half-filled d-subshell (d 5 ) and that of Zn is due to completely filled d-subshell (d 10 ). Highest oxide of Mn is Mn 2 O 7 because oxygen can form multiple bonds whereas fluorine can only form single bonds with metals therefore, highest fluoride of Mn is MnF 4 . {tex}2CrO_4^{2 - } + 2{H^ + } \to C{r_2}O_7^{2 - } + {H_2}O{/tex} On strong heating, KMn04 decomposes to potassium manganate. {tex}\begin{array}{l}{2 \mathrm{KMnO}_{4} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{4}+\mathrm{MnO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}} \\ {(\text { Purple })}\end{array}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Mn3+ / Mn2+ has large positive Eo value. Hence, Mn3+ can be easily reduced to Mn2+. Therefore, it is a good oxidising agent. Also, Mn2+ has half-filled electronic configuration, so it is more stable than Mn3+ state.
    2. There is decreasing negative electrode potentials of M2+ / M in the first transition series due to increase in the sum of IE1 and IE2. It shows that in general. the stability of +2 oxidation state decreases from left to right. Exceptions are Mn and Zn in which the greater stability of +2 state for Mn is due to half-filled d-subshell (d5) and that of Zn is due to completely filled d-subshell (d10).
    3. Highest oxide of Mn is Mn2O7 because oxygen can form multiple bonds whereas fluorine can only form single bonds with metals therefore, highest fluoride of Mn is MnF4.
    1. {tex}2CrO_4^{2 - } + 2{H^ + } \to C{r_2}O_7^{2 - } + {H_2}O{/tex}
    2. On strong heating, KMn04 decomposes to potassium manganate.
      {tex}\begin{array}{l}{2 \mathrm{KMnO}_{4} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{4}+\mathrm{MnO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}} \\ {(\text { Purple })}\end{array}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html: Explain the following observation: The members of the actinoid series exhibit a larger number of oxidation states than the corresponding members of the lanthanoid series.
question_text: Explain the following observation: The members of the actinoid series exhibit a larger number of oxidation states than the corresponding members of the lanthanoid series.
explanation: Actinoids have lower ionisation energy and less effective nuclear charge, hence more number of valence electrons can take part in bond formation. It is due to the fact that 5f, 6d and 7s levels are of comparable energies. Therefore, actinoids exhibit +3, +4, +5, +6and +7 oxidation states due to the participation of 5f, 6d and 7s electrons in bond formation. Hence, actinoids exhibit a greater range of oxidation states than lanthanoids.

Hence, actinoids exhibit a greater range of oxidation states than lanthanoids.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html: The metallic radii of the third (5d) series of transition metals are virtually the same as those of the corresponding group member of the second (4d) series. Give reason.
question_text: The metallic radii of the third (5d) series of transition metals are virtually the same as those of the corresponding group member of the second (4d) series. Give reason.
explanation: Metallic radii of third (5d) series of transition metals are virtually same as those of second (4d) series because of the lanthanoid contraction. This is associated with the intervention of the 4f-orbitals which are filled before the 5d- series of elements starts. The filling of 4f- orbitals before 5d-orbitals result in a regular decrease in atomic radii, called lanthanoid contraction which compensates the expected increase in atomic size with increasing atomic numbers.

This is associated with the intervention of the 4f-orbitals which are filled before the 5d- series of elements starts. The filling of 4f- orbitals before 5d-orbitals result in a regular decrease in atomic radii, called lanthanoid contraction which compensates the expected increase in atomic size with increasing atomic numbers.

subject: Chemistry

question_html: Explain the following observation: Most of the transition metal ions exhibit characteristic colours in aqueous solutions.
question_text: Explain the following observation: Most of the transition metal ions exhibit characteristic colours in aqueous solutions.
explanation: Most of the complexes of transition elements are coloured. This is because of the absorption of radiation from visible light region to promote an electron from one of the d-orbital to another. These are known as d-d transitions and are responsible for imparting colour to the solution. The ions of transition elements absorb the radiation of a particular wavelength and the rest is reflected, imparting colour to the solution.

question_html: How would you account for the following? Many of the transition elements are known to form interstitial compounds.
question_text: How would you account for the following? Many of the transition elements are known to form interstitial compounds.

question_text: How would you account for the following? Lanthanoids form primarily +3 ions, while the actinoids usually have higher oxidation states in their compounds, +4 or even +6 being typical.
explanation: The wide range of oxidation states of actinoids is attributed to the fact that the 5f, 6d, and 7s energy levels are of comparable energies. Therefore, all these three subshells can participate in bonding. But the most common oxidation state of actinoids is also +3.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html:
Explain the following observations:

  1. Many of the transition elements are known to form interstitial compounds.
  2. There is a general increase in density from titanium (Z = 22) to copper (Z = 29).
question_text: Explain the following observations: Many of the transition elements are known to form interstitial compounds. There is a general increase in density from titanium (Z = 22) to copper (Z = 29).
explanation: In the crystal lattice, transition metals exist in ccp and hcp structures. Transition elements have interstitial vacant spaces into which small-sized non-metal atoms such as H, B, C, or N are trapped. These compounds are known as interstitial compounds. These are neither typically ionic nor covalent, e.g. TiC, Mn 4 N, Fe 3 H, etc. As we move from left to right along a 1 st transition series (from Ti to Cu), the atomic radii decrease due to an increase in nuclear charge i.e, volume keeps decreasing but the mass increasing simultaneously. Therefore, atomic volume decreases with increase in atomic mass. As density = mass / volume, therefore, the density of transition metals increases from Titanium ( 22 Ti) to Copper ( 29 Cu).
solution: In the crystal lattice, transition metals exist in ccp and hcp structures. Transition elements have interstitial vacant spaces into which small-sized non-metal atoms such as H, B, C, or N are trapped. These compounds are known as interstitial compounds. These are neither typically ionic nor covalent, e.g. TiC, Mn 4 N, Fe 3 H, etc. As we move from left to right along a 1 st transition series (from Ti to Cu), the atomic radii decrease due to an increase in nuclear charge i.e, volume keeps decreasing but the mass increasing simultaneously. Therefore, atomic volume decreases with increase in atomic mass. As density = mass / volume, therefore, the density of transition metals increases from Titanium ( 22 Ti) to Copper ( 29 Cu).
solution_html:
1. In the crystal lattice, transition metals exist in ccp and hcp structures. Transition elements have interstitial vacant spaces into which small-sized non-metal atoms such as H, B, C, or N are trapped. These compounds are known as interstitial compounds. These are neither typically ionic nor covalent, e.g. TiC, Mn4N, Fe3H, etc.
  2. As we move from left to right along a 1st transition series (from Ti to Cu), the atomic radii decrease due to an increase in nuclear charge i.e, volume keeps decreasing but the mass increasing simultaneously. Therefore, atomic volume decreases with increase in atomic mass. 
    As density = mass / volume, therefore, the density of transition metals increases from Titanium (22Ti) to Copper (29Cu).

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html:
Explain each of the following observations:

  1. With the same d-orbital configuration (d4), Cr2+ is a reducing agent while Mn3+ ion is an oxidising agent.
  2. Manganese exhibits the hightest oxidation state of +7 among the 3d-series of transition elements.
question_text: Explain each of the following observations: With the same d-orbital configuration (d 4 ), Cr 2+ is a reducing agent while Mn 3+ ion is an oxidising agent. Manganese exhibits the hightest oxidation state of +7 among the 3d-series of transition elements.
explanation: Cr 2+ is a reducing agent because it can lose an electron to form Cr 3+ which has stable 3d 3 configuration (as it has half-filled t 2g level). On the other hand, Mn 3+ can accept an electron to form Mn 2+ resulting in the half-filled (d 5 ) configuration which has extra stability. Thus, it behaves as an oxidising agent. Manganese (Z = 25) exhibits largest number of oxidation states. This is because its electronic configuration is (3d 5 4s 2 ) and it has maximum number of electrons (five d electrons and two s electrons) to lose or share. Thus, it can exhibits an oxidation state of +2 to +7 which is the maximum number.
solution: Cr 2+ is a reducing agent because it can lose an electron to form Cr 3+ which has stable 3d 3 configuration (as it has half-filled t 2g level). On the other hand, Mn 3+ can accept an electron to form Mn 2+ resulting in the half-filled (d 5 ) configuration which has extra stability. Thus, it behaves as an oxidising agent. Manganese (Z = 25) exhibits largest number of oxidation states. This is because its electronic configuration is (3d 5 4s 2 ) and it has maximum number of electrons (five d electrons and two s electrons) to lose or share. Thus, it can exhibits an oxidation state of +2 to +7 which is the maximum number.

1. Cr2+ is a reducing agent because it can lose an electron to form Cr3+ which has stable 3d3 configuration (as it has half-filled t2g level). On the other hand, Mn3+ can accept an electron to form Mn2+ resulting in the half-filled (d5) configuration which has extra stability. Thus, it behaves as an oxidising agent.
  2. Manganese (Z = 25) exhibits largest number of oxidation states. This is because its electronic configuration is (3d54s2) and it has maximum number of electrons (five d electrons and two s electrons) to lose or share. Thus, it can exhibits an oxidation state of +2 to +7 which is the maximum number.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html:
1. Complete and balance the following chemical equations:
    1. {tex}C{r_2}O_7^{2 - } + {I^ - } + {H^ + } \to {/tex}
    2. {tex}MnO_4^ - + SO_3^{2 - } + {H^ + } \to {/tex}
  2. Explain the following observations:
    1. Transition elements and their compounds are known to act as catalysts.
    2. The higher oxidation states are usually exhibited by the members in the middle of a series of transition elements.
    3. The metal-metal bonding is more frequently found in the second and third series of transition elements.
question_text: Complete and balance the following chemical equations: {tex}C{r_2}O_7^{2 - } + {I^ - } + {H^ + } \to {/tex} {tex}MnO_4^ - + SO_3^{2 - } + {H^ + } \to {/tex} Explain the following observations: Transition elements and their compounds are known to act as catalysts. The higher oxidation states are usually exhibited by the members in the middle of a series of transition elements. The metal-metal bonding is more frequently found in the second and third series of transition elements.
explanation: {tex}C{r_2}O_7^{2 - } + 6{I^ - } + 14{H^ + } \to 2C{r^{3 + }} + 7{H_2}O + 3{I_2}{/tex} {tex}5SO_3^{2 - } + 2MnO_4^ - + 6{H^ + } \to 2M{n^{2 + }} + 3{H_2}O + 5SO_4^{2 - }{/tex} The most important reason transition metals are good catalysts is that they can lend electrons or withdraw electrons from the reagent, depending on the nature of the reaction. The ability of transition metals to be in a variety of oxidation states, the ability to interchange between the oxidation states and the ability to form complexes with the reagents and be a good source for electrons make transition metals good catalysts. Transition metals because of their variable valencies and vacant d-orbitals form unstable intermediate compounds and provide a new path with lower activation energy for the reaction. This is due to the presence of a large number of unpaired electrons in d-orbitals in the middle of the series and involvement of all ns and (n-1)d electrons in the bonding. e.g. Mn has oxidation states from +2 to +7. Metal-metal bonding is more frequent for 4d and 5d series of transition metals than that for 3d series. It is due to poor shielding effect of 4f and 5f -orbitals, more unpaired electrons take part in metallic bond formation.
solution: {tex}C{r_2}O_7^{2 - } + 6{I^ - } + 14{H^ + } \to 2C{r^{3 + }} + 7{H_2}O + 3{I_2}{/tex} {tex}5SO_3^{2 - } + 2MnO_4^ - + 6{H^ + } \to 2M{n^{2 + }} + 3{H_2}O + 5SO_4^{2 - }{/tex} The most important reason transition metals are good catalysts is that they can lend electrons or withdraw electrons from the reagent, depending on the nature of the reaction. The ability of transition metals to be in a variety of oxidation states, the ability to interchange between the oxidation states and the ability to form complexes with the reagents and be a good source for electrons make transition metals good catalysts. Transition metals because of their variable valencies and vacant d-orbitals form unstable intermediate compounds and provide a new path with lower activation energy for the reaction. This is due to the presence of a large number of unpaired electrons in d-orbitals in the middle of the series and involvement of all ns and (n-1)d electrons in the bonding. e.g. Mn has oxidation states from +2 to +7. Metal-metal bonding is more frequent for 4d and 5d series of transition metals than that for 3d series. It is due to poor shielding effect of 4f and 5f -orbitals, more unpaired electrons take part in metallic bond formation.
solution_html:
1. {tex}C{r_2}O_7^{2 - } + 6{I^ - } + 14{H^ + } \to 2C{r^{3 + }} + 7{H_2}O + 3{I_2}{/tex}
    2. {tex}5SO_3^{2 - } + 2MnO_4^ - + 6{H^ + } \to 2M{n^{2 + }} + 3{H_2}O + 5SO_4^{2 - }{/tex}
    1. The most important reason transition metals are good catalysts is that they can lend electrons or withdraw electrons from the reagent, depending on the nature of the reaction. The ability of transition metals to be in a variety of oxidation states, the ability to interchange between the oxidation states and the ability to form complexes with the reagents and be a good source for electrons make transition metals good catalysts. Transition metals because of their variable valencies and vacant d-orbitals form unstable intermediate compounds and provide a new path with lower activation energy for the reaction.
    2. This is due to the presence of a large number of unpaired electrons in d-orbitals in the middle of the series and involvement of all ns and (n-1)d electrons in the bonding. e.g. Mn has oxidation states from +2 to +7.
    3. Metal-metal bonding is more frequent for 4d and 5d series of transition metals than that for 3d series. It is due to poor shielding effect of 4f and 5f -orbitals, more unpaired electrons take part in metallic bond formation.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html:
Explain the following observations:

  1. The enthalpies of atomisation of transition elements are quite high.
  2. There occurs much more frequent metal-metal bonding in compounds of heavy transition metals (i.e. 4d and 5d series).
  3. Mn2+ is much more resistant than Fe2+ towards oxidation.
question_text: Explain the following observations: The enthalpies of atomisation of transition elements are quite high. There occurs much more frequent metal-metal bonding in compounds of heavy transition metals (i.e. 4d and 5d series). Mn 2+ is much more resistant than Fe 2+ towards oxidation.
explanation: Transition metals have high enthalpies of atomisation due to strong metallic bonding and additional covalent bonding. Metallic bonding is due to their smaller size while covalent bonding is due to d-d overlapping. Metal-metal bonding is more frequent for 4d and 5d series of transition metals than that for 3d series. It is due to poor shielding effect of 4f and 5f-orbitals, more unpaired electrons take part in metallic bond formation. Electronic configuration of Mn 2+ is 3d 5 which is half-filled and hence stable. Therefore, third ionisation enthalpy is very high, i.e. third electron cannot be lost easily. In case of Fe 2+ , electronic configuration is 3d 6 . Hence, it can lose one electron easily to give stable configuration, i.e. 3d 5 .
solution: Transition metals have high enthalpies of atomisation due to strong metallic bonding and additional covalent bonding. Metallic bonding is due to their smaller size while covalent bonding is due to d-d overlapping. Metal-metal bonding is more frequent for 4d and 5d series of transition metals than that for 3d series. It is due to poor shielding effect of 4f and 5f-orbitals, more unpaired electrons take part in metallic bond formation. Electronic configuration of Mn 2+ is 3d 5 which is half-filled and hence stable. Therefore, third ionisation enthalpy is very high, i.e. third electron cannot be lost easily. In case of Fe 2+ , electronic configuration is 3d 6 . Hence, it can lose one electron easily to give stable configuration, i.e. 3d 5 .
solution_html:
1. Transition metals have high enthalpies of atomisation due to strong metallic bonding and additional covalent bonding. Metallic bonding is due to their smaller size while covalent bonding is due to d-d overlapping.
  2. Metal-metal bonding is more frequent for 4d and 5d series of transition metals than that for 3d series. It is due to poor shielding effect of 4f and 5f-orbitals, more unpaired electrons take part in metallic bond formation.
  3. Electronic configuration of Mn2+ is 3d5 which is half-filled and hence stable. Therefore, third ionisation enthalpy is very high, i.e. third electron cannot be lost easily. In case of Fe2+, electronic configuration is 3d6. Hence, it can lose one electron easily to give stable configuration, i.e. 3d5.

question_html: The E0(M2+/M) value for copper is positive (+0.34 V). What is possibly the reason for this?
question_text: The E 0 (M 2+ /M) value for copper is positive (+0.34 V). What is possibly the reason for this?
explanation: This is because the high energy to transform Cu(s) to Cu 2+ (aq) is not balanced by its hydration enthalpy of Cu 2+ .
solution: This is because the high energy to transform Cu(s) to Cu 2+ (aq) is not balanced by its hydration enthalpy of Cu 2+ .
solution_html: This is because the high energy to transform Cu(s) to Cu2+(aq) is not balanced by its hydration enthalpy of Cu2+.

subject: Chemistry

question_html: What is lanthanoid contraction? What is its effect on the chemistry of the elements which follow the lanthanoids?
question_text: What is lanthanoid contraction? What is its effect on the chemistry of the elements which follow the lanthanoids?
explanation: The decrease in atomic and ionic size with increase in atomic number among lanthanoids is called lanthanoid contraction. The elements after lanthanoids closely resemble with the elements exactly above them due to similar ionic size for example Zr and Hf have similar sizes.
solution: The decrease in atomic and ionic size with increase in atomic number among lanthanoids is called lanthanoid contraction. The elements after lanthanoids closely resemble with the elements exactly above them due to similar ionic size for example Zr and Hf have similar sizes.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html:
Explain giving a suitable reason for each of the following:

  1. Transition metals and their compounds are generally found to be good catalysts.
  2. Metal-metal bonding is more frequent for the 4d and 5d-series transition metals than that for the 3d-series.
question_text: Explain giving a suitable reason for each of the following: Transition metals and their compounds are generally found to be good catalysts. Metal-metal bonding is more frequent for the 4d and 5d-series transition metals than that for the 3d-series.
explanation: Transition metals and their compounds function as catalysts either because of their ability to change oxidation state or, in the case of the metals, to adsorb other substances on to their surface and activate them in the process. Due to the small size, valence electrons of 3d metals are strongly bonded with the nucleus and thus they are not free for metal metal bonding. While in 4d and 5d elements the valence electrons are not so strongly bonded with nucleus and hence have greater tendency for metal metal bonding.
solution: Transition metals and their compounds function as catalysts either because of their ability to change oxidation state or, in the case of the metals, to adsorb other substances on to their surface and activate them in the process. Due to the small size, valence electrons of 3d metals are strongly bonded with the nucleus and thus they are not free for metal metal bonding. While in 4d and 5d elements the valence electrons are not so strongly bonded with nucleus and hence have greater tendency for metal metal bonding.
solution_html:
1. Transition metals and their compounds function as catalysts either because of their ability to change oxidation state or, in the case of the metals, to adsorb other substances on to their surface and activate them in the process.
  2. Due to the small size, valence electrons of 3d metals are strongly bonded with the nucleus and thus they are not free for metal metal bonding. While in 4d and 5d elements the valence electrons are not so strongly bonded with nucleus and hence have greater tendency for metal metal bonding.

  2. Oxidation states
  3. Chemical reactivity
question_text: Compare the chemistry of actinoids with that of lanthanoids with reference to: Electronic configuration Oxidation states Chemical reactivity
explanation: Electronic configuration : In lanthanoids 4f - orbitals are progressively filled so their electronic configuration is 4f 1-14 5d 0-1 6s 2 whereas in actinoids 5f-orbitals are progressively filled so their electronic configuration is 5f 1-14 6d 0-1 7s 2 Oxidation states: Lanthanoid show +3 oxidation state. Some elements show +2 and +4 oxidation states also. Actinoids show +2 ,+4, +5, +6, + 7 oxidation states. Although +3 and +4 are most common. Chemical reactivity: Actinoids are more reactive than lanthanoids due to bigger atomic size and lower ionization energy.

solution: Copper (I) ions are unstable in aqueous solution and undergo disproportionation. 2Cu + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu 2+ + Cu The stability of Cu 2+ (aq) rather than Cu + (aq) is due to the much more negative {tex}\Delta_{hyd}{/tex} H of Cu 2+ (aq) than Cu + . which compensates more for the second ionisation enthalpy of Cu.

2Cu+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu2+ + Cu
The stability of Cu2+ (aq) rather than Cu+ (aq) is due to the much more negative {tex}\Delta_{hyd}{/tex} H of Cu2+(aq) than Cu+. which compensates more for the second ionisation enthalpy of Cu.

explanation: The energy required to remove electron that is to form cationic species is more in 4d and 5d series because of greater effective nuclear charge which is due to lanthanoid contraction. Thus, 4d and 5d series metals generally do not form stable cationic species.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_html: Compare the stability of +2 oxidation state for all the elements of the first transition series.
question_text: Compare the stability of +2 oxidation state for all the elements of the first transition series.
explanation: Sc +3 Ti +1 +2 +3 +4 V +1 +2 +3 +4 +5 Cr +1 +2 +3 +4 +5 +6 Mn +1 +2 +3 +4 +5 +6 +7 Fe +1 +2 +3 +4 +5 +6 Co +1 +2 +3 +4 +5 Ni +1 +2 +3 +4 Cu +1 +2 +3 The elements which give the greatest number of oxidation states occur in or near the middle of the series. Manganese, for example, exhibits all the oxidation states from +2 to +7. The lesser number of oxidation states at the extreme ends stems from either too few electrons to lose or share (Sc, Ti) or too many d electrons (hence fewer orbitals available in which to share electrons with others) for higher valence (Cu, Zn). Thus, early in the series scandium(II) is virtually unknown and titanium (IV) is more stable than Ti(III) or Ti(II). At the other end, the only oxidation state of zinc is +2 (no d electrons are involved). The relative stability of the +2 oxidation state increases on moving from Sc to Zn. This is because on moving from top to bottom, it becomes more and more difficult to remove the third electron from the d-orbital.
solution: Sc +3 Ti +1 +2 +3 +4 V +1 +2 +3 +4 +5 Cr +1 +2 +3 +4 +5 +6 Mn +1 +2 +3 +4 +5 +6 +7 Fe +1 +2 +3 +4 +5 +6 Co +1 +2 +3 +4 +5 Ni +1 +2 +3 +4 Cu +1 +2 +3 The elements which give the greatest number of oxidation states occur in or near the middle of the series. Manganese, for example, exhibits all the oxidation states from +2 to +7. The lesser number of oxidation states at the extreme ends stems from either too few electrons to lose or share (Sc, Ti) or too many d electrons (hence fewer orbitals available in which to share electrons with others) for higher valence (Cu, Zn). Thus, early in the series scandium(II) is virtually unknown and titanium (IV) is more stable than Ti(III) or Ti(II). At the other end, the only oxidation state of zinc is +2 (no d electrons are involved). The relative stability of the +2 oxidation state increases on moving from Sc to Zn. This is because on moving from top to bottom, it becomes more and more difficult to remove the third electron from the d-orbital.
